title: Engineering Practice
description: Engineering and project skills from scratch for complete beginners (in progress): the universal habits behind building anything well — systems thinking and requirements, the design process, trade-offs, specs, measurement, debugging, testing, version control, technical communication, project management, safety and ethics, and modern AI-assisted workflows. Beginner-first readings, a diagram in nearly every lesson, MCQ practice, tiered easy to hard.

lessons.title: What Is a System?
lessons.content:
Think about a car. It has an engine, wheels, brakes, and a steering wheel. On their own, none of those parts can take you to the shops. Put them together so they work as a team, and suddenly you can drive. That teamwork is the whole idea behind a **system**.

A **system** is a set of parts that work together to do something the parts cannot do alone. A kitchen during a busy dinner rush is a system: cooks, ovens, orders, and waiters all working together to get meals out. A sports team is a system too, where the lineup and how players pass the ball often matter more than any single star.

Two things make a system more than just a pile of parts. The first is the **interfaces**: the connections where parts meet and hand things off to each other. In a car, the pedal connects to the brakes; in a kitchen, the order slip connects the waiter to the cook. The second is the **boundary**: the imaginary line that says where your system ends and the outside world (its **environment**) begins. The car is the system; the road, weather, and driver are its environment.

When parts combine, the system can behave in ways no single part does. We call this **emergent behavior**. A car *moving* is emergent: no bolt or wheel "moves you" by itself. The catch: because the parts are connected, fixing one part can quietly break another. That is why it pays to see the whole, not just the piece in front of you.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes what a 'system' is?
choices: The single most important part of a machine
A set of parts that work together to do something the parts cannot do alone
Any object that is large and complicated
A list of instructions for one person to follow
items.explanation: A system is parts working together toward a shared purpose, like an engine, wheels, and brakes making a car drive. It is not just one important part (the point is the teamwork), and size or complexity alone does not make something a system. A plain instruction list is not a set of connected parts.

items.prompt: A car can drive you somewhere even though no single bolt or wheel can move you by itself. What is this 'whole behaves differently than its parts' idea called?
choices: An interface
A boundary
Emergent behavior
An assumption
items.explanation: Emergent behavior is when the whole system does something none of its parts do alone, like a car moving. An interface is where parts connect, and a boundary is where the system ends; neither names the 'whole vs parts' effect. An assumption is something taken for granted, which is unrelated here.

items.prompt: In a kitchen during a dinner rush, the order slip that the waiter hands to the cook is an example of what?
choices: The boundary of the system
The environment outside the system
Emergent behavior of the kitchen
An interface, where two parts connect and hand something off
items.explanation: An interface is the connection point where parts meet and pass something between them, exactly what the order slip does between waiter and cook. The boundary is where the system ends, the environment is the outside world, and emergent behavior is what the whole produces; none of those describe a hand-off point.

items.prompt: You decide to study a coffee machine as your system. The kitchen counter, the wall outlet, and the person pressing the button are best described as what?
choices: The environment, outside the system's boundary
Parts inside the system
Interfaces inside the system
Emergent behaviors of the system
items.explanation: Anything outside the line you draw around your system is its environment. If the coffee machine is the system, the counter, outlet, and user sit in the environment. They are not internal parts or interfaces of the machine, and they are certainly not behaviors that emerge from it.

items.prompt: What does the 'boundary' of a system tell you?
choices: Which part of the system is the most expensive
How fast the system can run
Where the system ends and the outside environment begins
The total number of parts in the system
items.explanation: A boundary is the dividing line between what counts as your system and what counts as the outside world. It says nothing about cost, speed, or how many parts there are; those are separate questions. Drawing the boundary clearly is what lets you say what is 'in' versus 'out'.

items.prompt: Why is 'seeing the whole system' useful when you change one part?
choices: Because the biggest part is always the cause of any problem
Because parts are connected, so fixing one part can accidentally break another
Because parts never affect each other once assembled
Because a system always works better with fewer parts
items.explanation: Parts connect through interfaces, so a change in one place can ripple out and break something elsewhere; seeing the whole helps you catch that. It is false that parts never affect each other (the opposite is the danger), that the biggest part is always to blame, or that fewer parts is automatically better.

items.prompt: Why can a sports team with weaker individual players sometimes beat a team of stars?
choices: The number of players on each side is always different
Individual skill is the only thing that ever decides a game
A team is not a system, so teamwork does not apply
How the parts connect and work together can matter more than any single part
items.explanation: A team is a system, and how players pass and coordinate (the connections between parts) can produce results no single star delivers. Saying skill is the only factor ignores teamwork, the player counts are the same, and a team clearly is a system of parts working together.

items.prompt: Which of these is the clearest example of emergent behavior?
choices: A single battery sitting on a shelf
A clock telling the time, even though no single gear 'tells time' alone
A screw lying loose in a drawer
A wheel by itself rolling down a hill once pushed
items.explanation: Telling the time emerges only when the gears, hands, and spring work together; no lone gear does it. A battery on a shelf, a loose screw, and a single wheel are each just one part doing (or not doing) its own simple thing, with no whole-system behavior emerging.

items.prompt: An engineer says, 'Let's define the system of interest before we start.' What are they mainly trying to settle?
choices: Which single part should be painted first
How much the whole project will be sold for
Who on the team is the most experienced
Which parts are inside the boundary and which things are part of the environment
items.explanation: Defining the system of interest means drawing the boundary: deciding what is in (the parts you will work on) and what is out (the environment). It is not about paint order, sale price, or who is most experienced; those do not tell you where the system begins and ends.

lessons.title: From a Vague Wish to Clear Requirements
lessons.content:
Imagine telling a builder, "I want a nice kitchen." You will probably get something random and not what you pictured. Now imagine saying, "Counters at 90 cm high, an oven that fits a large baking tray, finished by June." Now you are far more likely to get what you wanted. The difference is **requirements**.

Before building anything, a **requirement** is a clear, specific statement of *what the thing must do or be*. Good requirements turn a vague wish ("I want a good website") into concrete, testable statements ("loads in under 2 seconds," "works on a phone"). They are the heart of getting the right result.

Requirements come in a few kinds, and telling them apart helps. A **functional** requirement says what the thing must *do*, an action: "let users reset their password." A **non-functional** requirement describes a *quality* of how well it does things: speed, reliability, cost, or safety, like "loads in under 2 seconds." A **constraint** is a fixed limit you cannot change: "the budget is $500" or "it must run on a phone." Finally, an **assumption** is something you are taking for granted without proof, such as "users will have an internet connection."

A common beginner trap is mixing these up, especially calling a quality (non-functional) a feature (functional). Another trap is leaving a requirement vague: "it should be fast" sounds fine but cannot be checked, because nobody knows what "fast" means. The fix, which we sharpen next, is to make every requirement specific enough to test.

items.prompt: What is a 'requirement' for a project?
choices: A rough guess about how long the project will take
A clear, specific statement of what the thing must do or be
The name of the person paying for the project
A drawing of the finished product's color scheme
items.explanation: A requirement states what the thing must do or be, clearly and specifically, so you can build the right result and later check it. A time guess, the sponsor's name, and a color drawing are all other things; none of them pin down what the product must actually accomplish.

items.prompt: Why is 'I want a good website' a poor requirement on its own?
choices: It is vague, so you cannot check whether it was actually met
It is too long and detailed to be useful
It names a specific technology too early
It states a fixed budget that cannot change
items.explanation: The word 'good' is vague, so there is no way to test whether the result counts as good; that is the core problem. It is the opposite of too detailed, it names no technology, and it states no budget. A useful version replaces 'good' with specific, checkable statements.

items.prompt: Classify this requirement: 'The app shall let a user reset their password.'
choices: Non-functional (a quality like speed or reliability)
A constraint (a fixed limit)
Functional (something the app must do)
An assumption (something taken for granted)
items.explanation: Resetting a password is an action the app must perform, so it is a functional requirement. It is not describing a quality such as speed (non-functional), it is not a fixed limit like a budget (constraint), and it is not something merely assumed to be true.

items.prompt: Classify this requirement: 'The page shall load in under 2 seconds.'
choices: Functional (an action it must do)
Non-functional (a quality of how well it performs)
A constraint (a fixed external limit)
An assumption (something taken for granted)
items.explanation: Loading speed describes how well the page performs, which is a quality, so it is non-functional. It is a common trap to call this functional, but it is not a new action the page does; it is about the quality of an action. It is also not a fixed limit you were handed, nor an assumption.

items.prompt: Classify this requirement: 'The total budget must not exceed $500.'
choices: Functional (an action the product does)
Non-functional (a performance quality)
A constraint (a fixed limit you cannot change)
An assumption (something taken for granted)
items.explanation: A fixed budget cap is a limit imposed on the project, so it is a constraint. It is not something the product does (functional) and not a quality of how it performs (non-functional). It is also stated as a hard rule, not merely assumed, so it is not an assumption.

items.prompt: Which is the clearest difference between a functional and a non-functional requirement?
choices: Functional is always written down; non-functional never is
Functional says what it must do; non-functional says how well it must do it
Functional is optional; non-functional is mandatory
There is no real difference between them
items.explanation: Functional requirements name actions (what it does), while non-functional ones describe qualities like speed or reliability (how well it does them). Both should be written down, neither is automatically optional or mandatory because of its type, and the two are genuinely different categories.

items.prompt: 'We assume every user will have a reliable internet connection.' What kind of statement is this?
choices: A functional requirement (an action to build)
A non-functional requirement (a quality to hit)
A constraint set by the budget
An assumption (something taken for granted without proof)
items.explanation: This is something taken as true without being guaranteed, which is exactly an assumption. It is not an action the product performs, not a measured quality of performance, and not a budget-imposed limit. Writing assumptions down matters because if one turns out false, the plan may break.

items.prompt: Which rewrite best turns the vague wish 'make it easy to use' into a usable requirement?
choices: It should feel friendly and modern
A new user shall complete sign-up in 3 steps or fewer
It must be the easiest app anyone has ever used
It should just be easy, like everyone expects
items.explanation: 'Complete sign-up in 3 steps or fewer' is specific and can be checked by counting steps. The others stay vague: 'friendly and modern', 'easiest ever', and 'just easy' all leave you with no clear way to tell whether the result passes or fails.

items.prompt: A teammate says, 'It should be fast, everyone knows what that means, so the requirement is fine.' What is the best response?
choices: Agreed, vague requirements are perfectly okay if they sound reasonable
Agreed, since speed is a quality it does not need to be specific
It is not fine yet; 'fast' is vague and must be made specific so it can be tested
It is fine because everyone will interpret 'fast' the same way
items.explanation: 'Fast' means different things to different people, so the requirement cannot be checked until it is made specific, such as 'loads in under 2 seconds'. Accepting a vague requirement, or assuming everyone agrees on 'fast', is the trap; qualities like speed especially need a concrete, testable number.

lessons.title: Good Requirements, Acceptance Tests & Traceability
lessons.content:
You now know what requirements are. This lesson is about making them *good*, and proving they were met.

A **good requirement** passes a quick checklist. It is **clear** (no room for two readings), **verifiable** (you can test whether it is met), **atomic** (it states one thing, not five), **feasible** (actually possible), and **necessary** (you really need it). A vague line like "it should be fast" fails because it is not verifiable.

A big pitfall is **solution-bias**: writing *how* to build something instead of *what* it must achieve. "Shall show the time remaining" says what (good); "shall use a red LED display" says how (bad), because it locks in one solution before you have compared options. Compare "get to work by 9" (the real need) with "buy a red sports car" (one premature solution). Always state *what*, not *how*.

Requirements use careful words. **Shall** means it is mandatory (a must). **Should** means preferred but not required. **May** means optional. Mixing these up, like thinking "should" means mandatory, leads to building the wrong things.

Every requirement also needs an **acceptance test**: a concrete way to prove it is met. For "loads in under 2 seconds," the test is "time the load and confirm it is under 2 seconds." Finally, **traceability** means linking each requirement to the design choice that addresses it and the test that proves it, like a checklist where every must-have points to its proof, so nothing is forgotten. The same idea scales to **budgets as requirements**: one big limit, such as a $500 total or a 2-second load time, is split among the parts. Together these habits stop you from building the wrong thing well.

items.prompt: Which set of qualities describes a GOOD requirement?
choices: Long, detailed, and full of technical words
Vague enough to allow any interpretation later
Clear, verifiable, atomic, feasible, and necessary
Written only after the product is finished
items.explanation: A good requirement is clear, verifiable (testable), atomic (one thing), feasible, and necessary. Length and jargon do not make it good, vagueness makes it worse (you cannot test it), and requirements are written before building, not after.

items.prompt: What does it mean for a requirement to be 'verifiable'?
choices: There is a concrete way to test whether it has been met
It has been approved by a manager
It sounds reasonable to most people
It is written in formal-sounding language
items.explanation: Verifiable means you can actually test it and get a yes or no, which is why specific, measurable wording matters. Manager approval, sounding reasonable, or using formal language do not make a requirement testable; only a concrete check does. Thinking an untestable requirement is acceptable is the trap to avoid.

items.prompt: Which requirement shows solution-bias (says HOW instead of WHAT)?
choices: The timer shall show the time remaining
The timer shall be readable from across the room
The timer shall use a red LED display
The timer shall start when the start button is pressed
items.explanation: 'Use a red LED display' dictates one specific solution (the how) rather than the goal, which is solution-bias. The others state what must be achieved (show time remaining, be readable, start on a press) and leave the designer free to pick the best way to do it.

items.prompt: Why is it risky to lock in a solution too early inside a requirement?
choices: It always makes the product cost less
It can rule out better solutions you have not considered yet
It guarantees the project finishes faster
It makes the requirement easier to test
items.explanation: Stating how (a specific solution) too early closes the door on potentially better options, which is the main danger of solution-bias. It does not reliably cut cost, speed up the project, or make testing easier; in fact, a 'what' requirement is usually just as testable and keeps your options open.

items.prompt: In requirement language, what does 'shall' mean?
choices: It is optional, take it or leave it
It is only a preference, not required
It is a guess about the future
It is mandatory, a must
items.explanation: 'Shall' marks a mandatory requirement, something that must be done. 'May' marks optional, and 'should' marks a preference that is not required. None of these words is a guess about the future; they set how binding each requirement is.

items.prompt: A teammate treats every 'should' in the spec as a strict must-have. Why is that a mistake?
choices: 'Should' means preferred, not mandatory; only 'shall' is mandatory
'Should' actually means optional, weaker than 'may'
'Should' and 'shall' mean exactly the same thing
'Should' means the requirement can be ignored entirely
items.explanation: 'Should' signals a preference that is not strictly required, while 'shall' is the mandatory word; treating 'should' as a must can waste effort on non-essentials. 'Should' is stronger than the optional 'may', it is not identical to 'shall', and it is not something to ignore outright.

items.prompt: What is an 'acceptance test' for a requirement?
choices: A meeting where the team votes on the design
A concrete way to prove the requirement has been met
A guess about whether users will like the product
The first draft of the requirement before edits
items.explanation: An acceptance test is the concrete check that proves a requirement was satisfied, such as timing a page load to confirm it is under 2 seconds. It is not a vote, a guess about user reactions, or a draft; it is the evidence that the requirement is truly met.

items.prompt: What does 'traceability' mean in requirements work?
choices: Tracking how many hours each person worked
Keeping a copy of every email about the project
Linking each requirement to the design choice addressing it and the test proving it
Drawing the product to exact scale
items.explanation: Traceability links every requirement to the design decision that addresses it and the test that proves it, so nothing gets forgotten, like a checklist tying each must-have to its proof. Timesheets, saved emails, and scale drawings are unrelated to connecting requirements through design to test.

items.prompt: You have a hard limit of $500 for a whole project. How does the idea of 'budgets as requirements' apply?
choices: Spend the full $500 on whichever part you build first
Ignore the limit until the project is finished
Treat the $500 as a vague suggestion, not a real limit
Split the $500 limit among the parts so each part gets its own share
items.explanation: A big limit is decomposed into smaller shares across the parts, just as a 2-second load time can be split across steps; that keeps the whole within bounds. Spending it all on the first part, ignoring the limit, or treating a hard cap as a mere suggestion all break the budget as a requirement.

lessons.title: Design Is a Loop, Not a Straight Line
lessons.content:
Think about cooking a new recipe. You rarely nail it on the first try. You cook a version, taste it, decide it needs more salt, adjust, and cook it again. That try-taste-adjust cycle is the whole idea behind the **engineering design process**: a repeatable, step-by-step path that takes you from a problem to a working solution.

The key surprise for beginners is that this path is a **loop, not a straight line**. You do not march once from start to finish. You go around the cycle, learn something, and come back to improve. Designing a treehouse works the same way: you plan it, build a bit, notice the floor wobbles, and rethink part of the design.

The usual stages are: **understand the problem** (you already know this means pinning down what it must do, in testable terms), **imagine many possible ideas**, **choose** one to pursue, **design it in detail**, build a rough **prototype** (an early, cheap version), **test** it, **learn** from what fails, and then **iterate** (refine and repeat). "Iterate" simply means going around the loop again with what you learned.

Why does this matter? Because first attempts are *meant* to be improved. A real design loops back many times before it is good. If you expect to get everything right in one straight shot, you will be surprised, frustrated, and usually wrong. Expecting to circle back is not failure; it is how good things get made.

items.prompt: What is the engineering design process?
choices: A repeatable, step-by-step path from a problem to a working solution
A single drawing of the finished product
A rule that the first idea must always be built
A list of who is in charge of the project
items.explanation: The design process is a repeatable series of steps that carries you from a problem to a working solution. A single drawing is just one artifact, building the first idea no matter what is the opposite of good practice, and naming who is in charge is about roles, not the process itself.

items.prompt: Is the engineering design process best described as linear (a straight line) or iterative (a loop)?
choices: Linear: you finish each step once and never return to it
Iterative: you loop back to improve the design many times
Linear: it always ends at the first prototype
Neither: the order of steps does not matter at all
items.explanation: Real design is iterative: you go around the loop, learn, and come back to refine, often many times. Treating it as a straight line you finish once is the classic beginner mistake. The steps do follow a sensible order, so it is not true that order never matters.

items.prompt: Cooking a new recipe by tasting and adjusting until it is right is a good everyday picture of what?
choices: Building the final product perfectly on the very first try
Skipping testing because you already know the answer
Looping through build, test, and adjust to improve the result
Writing requirements and never changing anything afterward
items.explanation: Taste-and-adjust is exactly the build-test-improve loop at the heart of the design process. It is the opposite of getting it perfect on the first try, it clearly involves testing (tasting), and it shows things changing as you learn rather than being fixed forever.

items.prompt: In the design loop, what does it mean to 'iterate'?
choices: To delete the project and start a brand-new unrelated one
To stop all work once the first version exists
To hand the project to someone else to finish
To go around the loop again, refining the design with what you learned
items.explanation: Iterating means repeating the cycle, using what you learned to refine the design. It is not throwing everything away for an unrelated project, not stopping at the first version, and not handing the work off; it is deliberate, informed improvement.

items.prompt: You already learned the first stage of design from requirements work. What does 'understand the problem' mainly involve?
choices: Choosing the paint color before anything else
Pinning down what the thing must do, in clear and testable terms
Building a finished version as fast as possible
Guessing the price and moving on
items.explanation: Understanding the problem means nailing down what the result must do, stated clearly enough to test later, which connects directly to the requirements work you have done. Picking paint, rushing to a finished build, or guessing a price all skip the real job of defining the need.

items.prompt: Which sequence shows the usual flow of the design loop?
choices: Build first, then decide what problem you were solving
Test, ship, then think about requirements afterward
Understand, imagine ideas, choose, design, prototype, test, learn, iterate
Pick the first idea, build it fully, and never test it
items.explanation: The usual flow is understand the problem, imagine many ideas, choose one, design it, build a prototype, test, learn, and iterate. Building before knowing the problem, leaving requirements until after shipping, or committing to the first idea with no testing all break the sensible order.

items.prompt: Planning a big event by sketching a plan, trying part of it, spotting problems, and adjusting is an example of what?
choices: A one-shot straight line with no chance to adjust
An iterative loop, where you circle back to improve
Proof that planning is a waste of time
A reason to never test anything before the event
items.explanation: Trying part of a plan, finding problems, and adjusting is looping back to improve, which is iteration. It is not a one-shot line, it does not show planning is wasteful, and it actually relies on testing parts early rather than avoiding tests.

items.prompt: Why is it healthy to expect your first attempt at a design to need changes?
choices: Because changing anything later means the project has failed
Because first attempts are meant to be improved as you learn
Because the first attempt is always the best you can do
Because good designers never test their early versions
items.explanation: Good design loops back many times, so first attempts are starting points meant to be refined as you learn. Believing change equals failure, that the first try is automatically best, or that early versions should not be tested all misunderstand how real design works.

items.prompt: A beginner says, 'I will design it perfectly on paper, build it once, and be done.' What is the main flaw in this thinking?
choices: Real design loops back to improve, so expecting one perfect straight pass is unrealistic
Nothing is wrong; design really is a single straight line
The only flaw is that paper is the wrong material to use
The flaw is spending any time understanding the problem first
items.explanation: The flaw is treating design as a single straight pass when it is really an iterative loop that improves over many cycles. Design is not a one-shot line, the issue is not the paper itself, and understanding the problem first is a strength, not a flaw.

lessons.title: Imagine Many Ideas, Then Build a Rough Prototype
lessons.content:
Imagine you need a logo for a club. If you sketch one idea and stop, you will probably miss a better one. So good designers first **diverge**: they generate lots of different ideas on purpose. Only afterward do they **converge**: they compare the options and narrow down to the one to pursue.

Diverging means widening your options before choosing. Converging means narrowing to a single choice. Planning a trip works the same way: list many possible destinations first, then pick. The trap to avoid is **picking the first idea you think of**. Your first idea is rarely your best; reaching for more ideas before deciding is what uncovers the good ones. A handy reminder: *don't fall in love with your first idea.*

Once you have chosen a direction, the next key habit is **prototyping**. A **prototype** is a rough, cheap, early version built to answer a question, not to be the final product. It can be a paper sketch of an app screen, a cardboard model of a gadget, or a small test batch before you cook for a crowd. It just has to be good enough to learn something.

Why build rough early instead of perfecting on paper? Because a prototype lets you **learn fast and find problems while they are cheap to fix**. Perfecting every detail on paper before you test feels productive, but it hides flaws until later, when they are far more expensive to undo. The motto is **fail fast, fail cheap**: discover what is wrong early, on something quick and disposable, then improve.

items.prompt: What does it mean to 'diverge' early in the design process?
choices: To commit to a single idea right away
To generate many different possible ideas on purpose
To stop having ideas and start building
To delete ideas until only the first one is left
items.explanation: Diverging means deliberately widening your options by generating many ideas before you choose. Committing to one idea immediately, halting ideas to start building, or trimming down to just the first idea are all forms of converging too early or skipping the idea-generation step.

items.prompt: Why is it better to diverge (make many ideas) before you converge (pick one)?
choices: Because the first idea you think of is usually the best
Because more ideas always cost more money to build
Because generating many options first helps you find better ideas you would otherwise miss
Because picking quickly is the main goal of design
items.explanation: Exploring many options before choosing surfaces strong ideas you would miss if you stopped at the first one. The first idea is rarely the best, generating ideas is cheap (you do not build them all), and the goal is a good result, not merely a fast pick.

items.prompt: Why is 'just pick the first idea you think of' a risky habit?
choices: It usually skips better ideas you would have found by exploring more
It takes far too long compared to other methods
It always produces the cheapest possible design
It is the recommended way to do professional design
items.explanation: Grabbing the first idea tends to lock you onto a weaker option and skip better ones you would find by diverging first. It is not slow (it is too fast), it does not guarantee the cheapest design, and it is the opposite of recommended practice; remember, don't fall in love with your first idea.

items.prompt: What is a 'prototype'?
choices: The final, polished product ready to sell
A written list of requirements
A meeting where the team votes on ideas
A rough, cheap, early version built to answer a question
items.explanation: A prototype is an early, rough, inexpensive version made to learn something, not the finished product. It is not the polished final item, not a requirements list, and not a meeting; its whole point is to be quick and disposable enough to test an idea.

items.prompt: Which of these is the best example of an early prototype?
choices: A fully manufactured product shipped to stores
A paper sketch of an app screen used to test the layout
A signed contract with a supplier
A finished user manual for the product
items.explanation: A paper sketch of a screen is a cheap, rough version made just to test an idea, which is exactly a prototype. A shipped product is the final result, and a contract or a finished manual are documents, not quick test versions you build to learn early.

items.prompt: Why build a rough prototype early instead of perfecting the design on paper first?
choices: Because paper designs are always wrong and useless
Because a prototype lets you find problems early, when they are cheap to fix
Because prototypes are meant to be the final product
Because testing should only happen at the very end of a project
items.explanation: A rough prototype lets you learn fast and catch problems while they are still cheap and easy to fix. Paper plans are useful but can hide flaws, a prototype is not the final product, and waiting until the very end to test is exactly what prototyping helps you avoid.

items.prompt: What does the motto 'fail fast, fail cheap' encourage you to do?
choices: Avoid ever making mistakes by planning forever
Discover problems early on something quick and disposable, then improve
Spend as much as possible perfecting the first version
Wait until the product is finished before checking if it works
items.explanation: Fail fast, fail cheap means surfacing problems early using quick, low-cost versions so mistakes are cheap to fix. Planning forever delays learning, perfecting the first version is expensive and risky, and waiting until the end to check is the costly trap this motto warns against.

items.prompt: A baker plans to cook 200 portions of a brand-new dish for an event. What is the smartest prototyping move?
choices: Cook all 200 at once and hope it tastes right
Skip tasting since the recipe is written down carefully
Make a small test batch first, taste it, and adjust before scaling up
Wait until the event to find out if guests like it
items.explanation: A small test batch is a cheap prototype: it lets the baker find and fix problems before committing to all 200 portions. Cooking everything at once, trusting the written recipe without tasting, or waiting until the event all risk a large, expensive failure that a quick test would have caught.

items.prompt: A teammate says, 'Let's not waste time on a rough mock-up; we'll perfect the whole design on paper before we build anything.' What is the best response?
choices: Agreed, perfecting everything on paper first is the safest approach
A quick prototype would actually help us find problems early, while they are cheap to fix
Agreed, prototypes are only for the final version of a product
We should just build the first idea fully and skip the mock-up
items.explanation: A rough prototype reveals problems early, when fixing them is cheap, which is exactly why it is worth a little time. Perfecting everything on paper hides flaws until they are costly, prototypes are early learning tools rather than final versions, and rushing to fully build the first idea skips the cheap learning a mock-up provides.

lessons.title: Iterate, Test Against Requirements, and the V-Model
lessons.content:
You have a loop and a prototype. This lesson is about what powers the loop: **feedback**, and how you know when you are done.

Each trip around the loop is a **build-test-learn cycle**. You build a version, test it, learn from what fails, and feed that lesson into the next build. Editing many drafts of an essay is the same: each draft is better because the last one showed you what to fix.

A crucial truth drives *when* you test: the **cost of change rises the later you catch a flaw**. A problem spotted in an early sketch is cheap to fix; the same problem found after everything is built is expensive, because more work now depends on it. This is exactly why prototyping early and looping often pays off: you catch flaws while they are still cheap.

How do you know the design is good enough? You **test it against the requirements**. Recall that a good requirement comes with an **acceptance test**, a concrete check that proves it is met. Testing against those tells you one clear thing: whether the design does what you asked, and therefore whether you are done. If a test fails, you iterate; if all pass, the design meets its requirements.

A simple picture for this is the **V-model**. You plan and design on the way *down* the left side, you build at the bottom, then you verify and test on the way *up* the right side, and **every requirement should have a matching test that checks it** (this is the **traceability** idea you met before). One more habit fits here: a **design review** is pausing to let others check your work before you commit to it, catching problems while they are still cheap.

items.prompt: What is a 'build-test-learn' cycle?
choices: Building the final product once and never testing it
A way to avoid ever changing the design
A meeting held only after the product ships
Building a version, testing it, and using what you learn to improve the next version
items.explanation: A build-test-learn cycle means you build, test, and feed the lessons into the next version, which is how iteration improves a design. Building once with no testing, avoiding change, or holding a post-ship meeting all miss the point: the cycle is about learning and refining as you go.

items.prompt: Why is fixing a flaw later in a project usually more expensive than fixing it early?
choices: Because late fixes are always free
Because more work has been built on top of the flaw, so changing it costs more
Because early fixes require buying special tools
Because the cost of fixing a flaw never changes over time
items.explanation: The later a flaw is caught, the more finished work depends on it, so undoing and reworking that costs more. Late fixes are not free, early fixes do not require special tools, and the whole point is that cost does change, rising the longer a flaw goes unnoticed.

items.prompt: A teammate claims, 'It costs the same to fix a problem now as it will after we ship.' Why is this wrong?
choices: It is correct; cost of change is flat over time
Because problems found after shipping are actually cheaper to fix
Because the cost of change rises the later a flaw is found, so fixing it after shipping costs more
Because shipping makes all problems disappear
items.explanation: The cost of change climbs over time, so a flaw fixed after shipping is more expensive than the same flaw caught early. Cost is not flat, late problems are not cheaper, and shipping does not erase problems; this is precisely why early testing and prototyping save money.

items.prompt: What does testing your design against its requirements tell you?
choices: Which team member worked the hardest
Whether the design does what you asked, and therefore whether you are done
How much the project will sell for
Only what color the product should be
items.explanation: Testing against the requirements checks whether the design actually does what was asked, which is how you judge whether it is finished. It says nothing about who worked hardest, the sale price, or color choices; those are unrelated to whether requirements are met.

items.prompt: When a test against a requirement fails, what should you do next?
choices: Ship the product anyway, since testing is only a formality
Iterate: learn from the failure and refine the design, then test again
Delete the requirement so the test passes
Assume late changes are just as cheap, so wait until the very end
items.explanation: A failed test is feedback that sends you around the loop again to refine and retest. Shipping anyway ignores the problem, deleting the requirement hides it rather than meeting the real need, and assuming late changes are cheap is false because cost of change rises over time.

items.prompt: In the V-model, what happens on the way DOWN the left side versus UP the right side?
choices: Down is testing; up is planning
Both sides are only about choosing a color scheme
There is no order; the sides mean nothing
Down is planning and design; up is building and then verifying with tests
items.explanation: The V-model goes down the left through planning and design, reaches build at the bottom, then comes up the right verifying with tests. Testing is not on the way down, the model is about design and verification rather than color, and the two sides do carry a clear meaning and order.

items.prompt: The V-model captures one simple rule about requirements and tests. What is it?
choices: Tests are optional and only run if there is spare time
Every requirement should have a matching test that checks it
One test at the end can cover every requirement at once
Requirements and tests have nothing to do with each other
items.explanation: The core idea is traceability: each requirement should have a matching test that verifies it does what was asked. Tests are not optional extras, a single end-of-project test rarely proves every requirement, and requirements and tests are tightly linked, not unrelated.

items.prompt: Editing an essay through several drafts, each better because the last revealed what to fix, is a good picture of what design idea?
choices: A one-shot straight line with no revisions
Iteration through build-test-learn feedback loops
Proof that you should never get feedback on your work
A reason to finish everything before checking it once
items.explanation: Each draft improving on the last is iteration powered by feedback, the same build-test-learn loop used in design. It is not a single straight pass, it relies on feedback rather than avoiding it, and it shows checking work along the way instead of only at the end.

items.prompt: What is a 'design review'?
choices: A pause to let others check your work before you commit to it
A test that runs automatically after shipping
A rule that forbids changing the design ever again
A guess about how much customers will like the product
items.explanation: A design review is when you pause and have others check your work before committing, catching problems while they are still cheap to fix. It is not a post-ship automatic test, not a ban on future changes, and not a guess about customer reactions.

lessons.title: You Can't Have It All: Trade-offs
lessons.content:
You already know how design works: you come up with many ideas, then you have to **choose** one. This lesson is about the hard truth behind that choice. Most of the time, you cannot make everything great at once. Improving one thing usually costs you another. That is a **trade-off**: a choice where getting more of one quality means accepting less of another.

Think about buying a phone. You might want it thin, with huge battery life, and cheap. But a bigger battery makes it thicker, and a thinner cheaper phone holds less charge. You can't max out all three. Cars are the same: sporty, fuel-efficient, and roomy pull against each other. A famous shorthand sums it up: **fast, cheap, good — pick two**. You can usually have any two, but not all three together.

Why does this matter? Because the real skill is not wishing for a perfect option that has no downsides. That option rarely exists. The skill is choosing **deliberately**: knowing what you are giving up and deciding it is worth it. A beginner mistake is to assume you can optimize everything at once, or to pick on a gut feeling and only later notice what you sacrificed.

So when you choose, expect to give something up. That is not failure; it is the nature of real choices. Naming the trade-off out loud — 'I am paying more to get it sooner' — turns a blind guess into an informed decision.

items.prompt: What is a 'trade-off'?
choices: A choice where getting more of one quality means accepting less of another
A way to make every quality of a product great at the same time
A drawing of the finished product
A rule that the cheapest option is always best
items.explanation: A trade-off means improving one thing costs you another, like a bigger battery making a phone thicker. It is not a way to maximize everything at once (that is the misconception this whole topic warns against), it is not a drawing, and it does not declare the cheapest option the winner.

items.prompt: A friend says, 'I'll find a phone that is the thinnest, has the longest battery life, and is the cheapest, all at once.' What is the flaw in this thinking?
choices: There is no flaw; you can usually maximize every quality at the same time
The flaw is that thin phones are always the best ones
The flaw is assuming you can optimize everything at once, when these qualities trade off against each other
The flaw is caring about price at all
items.explanation: Thinness, battery life, and price pull against each other, so you cannot max them all out; expecting to is the core beginner mistake. You cannot usually maximize everything at once, thin is not automatically best, and caring about price is perfectly reasonable.

items.prompt: The saying 'fast, cheap, good — pick two' is meant to teach what idea?
choices: That speed is the only thing that ever matters
That you can usually have two of those qualities but not all three at once
That good work is impossible to achieve
That you should always refuse to choose
items.explanation: 'Pick two' captures that these three qualities trade off, so you typically get two and give up the third. It does not say speed is all that matters, that good work is impossible, or that you should never choose; the point is that choosing means giving something up.

items.prompt: Choosing a car that is sporty AND very fuel-efficient AND extremely roomy, all at the top level, is hard mainly because:
choices: Car colors are limited
Sportiness, fuel efficiency, and roominess tend to trade off against one another
Roomy cars are always the cheapest
Fuel efficiency does not matter to anyone
items.explanation: These qualities pull in different directions, so pushing one to the top usually costs another; that is a trade-off. Color choice is unrelated, roomy cars are not automatically cheapest, and fuel efficiency clearly matters to many buyers.

items.prompt: What does it mean to make a choice 'deliberately' when trade-offs are involved?
choices: To pick instantly on a gut feeling and move on
To insist on an option with no downsides at all
To know what you are giving up and decide it is worth it
To let someone else choose so you avoid responsibility
items.explanation: Deliberate choosing means seeing the downside you are accepting and judging it worthwhile. Picking on gut feeling ignores what you sacrifice, insisting on a no-downside option ignores that trade-offs exist, and handing off the choice avoids the decision rather than making it well.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes what usually happens when you choose between real options?
choices: You give something up to gain something else
You always find an option that beats every other on every measure
You never have to compromise if you look hard enough
The first option you see is always the best
items.explanation: Real choices almost always involve giving something up to gain something else; that is the heart of a trade-off. An option that beats all others on every measure rarely exists, looking harder does not erase trade-offs, and the first option is not automatically best.

items.prompt: Saying out loud, 'I am paying more so I can have it sooner,' is a good habit because it:
choices: Proves that price never matters
Hides the downside of the choice
Guarantees you made the cheapest choice
Names the trade-off, turning a blind guess into an informed decision
items.explanation: Naming what you give up (money) for what you gain (speed) makes the decision conscious and informed. It does not prove price never matters, it reveals rather than hides the downside, and it does not guarantee the cheapest choice; in fact it openly chooses to pay more.

items.prompt: A lighter backpack frame is more comfortable to carry but bends more easily, while a heavier frame is sturdier. This is an example of:
choices: A trade-off between weight and sturdiness
A product with no downsides
Proof that heavier is always better
A choice that involves giving up nothing
items.explanation: Lighter versus sturdier is a classic trade-off: improving comfort (less weight) costs strength. It is not a no-downside product, heavier is not always better (it is just a different balance), and the example clearly involves giving something up.

items.prompt: Why is expecting to 'give something up' when you choose NOT a sign of failure?
choices: Because failure only happens when a product is cheap
Because trade-offs are the normal nature of real choices, so a downside is expected, not a mistake
Because good choices actually have zero downsides
Because giving something up means you chose wrong
items.explanation: Since you usually cannot maximize everything, accepting a downside is the normal result of any real choice, not a failure. Cheapness does not define failure, good choices still have downsides, and giving something up is expected rather than proof of a wrong choice.

lessons.title: Making It Explicit: Criteria, Weights, and a Decision Matrix
lessons.content:
Knowing that trade-offs exist is step one. Step two is comparing your options **on purpose** instead of by gut. The tool for this is simple and powerful.

Start by listing the **criteria** — the things that matter for this choice. For a phone that might be price, battery life, and camera quality. Next, decide which criteria matter **most**. Those are your **weights**: a number showing how important each one is. An everyday version is a pros-and-cons list where you put stars next to the things you care about most.

Now build a **decision matrix** (also called a trade study or Pugh chart). It is just a table: your **options are the rows**, your **criteria are the columns**. You **score** each option on each criterion (say 1 to 5, where 5 is best), multiply each score by that criterion's weight, and add up the row. The option with the highest total is a **defensible choice** — one you can explain with numbers, not just a feeling.

Here is one clean example. Suppose price matters most (weight 3) and battery is next (weight 2). Phone B scores 3 on price and 5 on battery: that is 3×3 plus 5×2, which equals 9 plus 10, so 19 points. If a rival phone totals 16, Phone B wins on the criteria you said mattered.

One more rule belongs here. Some requirements are not scored at all — they are **hard constraints**, must-haves that an option either meets or fails. A car that won't fit your garage is out no matter how many points it earns. Check constraints first; score what survives.

items.prompt: In a decision matrix, what are the 'criteria'?
choices: The people who will use the product
The things that matter for the choice, such as price, battery, or quality
The single number that decides the winner
The leftover options you already rejected
items.explanation: Criteria are the qualities that matter for the decision, like price and battery, which become the columns you compare options against. They are not the users, not the final total by itself, and not the rejected options.

items.prompt: What does a 'weight' represent in weighted scoring?
choices: How heavy the physical product is
The price of the cheapest option
How important a criterion is compared to the others
The number of options being compared
items.explanation: A weight is a number showing how much a criterion matters relative to the others, so important things count more in the total. It is not the product's physical weight, not a price, and not a count of options.

items.prompt: What does a decision matrix actually do for you?
choices: It organizes options and weighted scores so you can compare them and pick a defensible winner
It removes the need to use any judgment at all
It guarantees the perfect answer no matter what numbers you put in
It automatically lowers the price of every option
items.explanation: A decision matrix lays out options (rows) and weighted criteria (columns) so you can compare totals and defend your pick with numbers. It supports judgment rather than replacing it, it does not guarantee a perfect answer (bad weights give bad results), and it has nothing to do with changing prices.

items.prompt: In a decision matrix, how are the options and criteria usually arranged?
choices: Options and criteria are both squeezed into a single column
Options are the rows and criteria are the columns
There is no table; you just pick by feel
Criteria are the rows and the winner is hidden
items.explanation: The standard layout puts each option on its own row and each criterion in its own column, with scores filling the grid. Cramming everything into one column, using no table, or hiding the winner all defeat the purpose of organizing the comparison.

items.prompt: Price has weight 3 and battery has weight 2. Phone B scores 3 on price and 5 on battery. What is its weighted total?
choices: 8 (just adding the two scores)
10 (only the battery part)
5 (the highest single score)
items.explanation: You multiply each score by its weight and add: 3×3 is 9, 5×2 is 10, and 9 plus 10 is 19. Adding raw scores (8) ignores the weights, using only battery (10) drops price entirely, and taking the highest single score (5) is not how totals work.

items.prompt: Why is the option with the highest weighted total called a 'defensible' choice?
choices: Because it is always the cheapest one
Because you can explain it with the criteria and scores, not just a gut feeling
Because no one is allowed to question it
Because it was the first option on the list
items.explanation: It is defensible because you can point to the criteria, weights, and scores that justify it, instead of saying 'I just liked it.' It is not necessarily cheapest, it can still be questioned and revised, and its position on the list is irrelevant.

items.prompt: An everyday pros-and-cons list with stars next to the points you care about most is basically a simple version of what?
choices: A criteria-and-weights comparison (a decision matrix)
A random guess with no structure
A way to ignore what matters to you
A rule that the longest list always wins
items.explanation: Stars marking what matters most are weights, and the pros and cons are criteria, so the list is a plain decision matrix. It is not a random guess, it highlights rather than ignores what matters, and the winner is about weighted importance, not list length.

items.prompt: A scored criterion adds points to an option's total. A hard constraint is different because it:
choices: Adds the most points of all
Is a must-have that disqualifies any option failing it, no matter the score
Is just a criterion with a slightly bigger weight
Can be ignored if the total score is high enough
items.explanation: A hard constraint is a pass-or-fail must-have: fail it and the option is out regardless of points, like a car that won't fit the garage. It is not about adding points, it is not merely a heavier-weighted criterion, and a high score does not let you ignore it.

items.prompt: An apartment scores the highest in your matrix, but it does not allow the pet you already own. Pets allowed is a firm must-have. What should you do?
choices: Pick it anyway, since the total score is highest
Raise its score even higher to settle the matter
Treat 'pets allowed' as a hard constraint that disqualifies it, and choose from the options that pass
Delete the pet requirement so the apartment can win
items.explanation: A firm must-have like pets allowed is a hard constraint, so failing it removes the apartment no matter how high it scores; you choose among options that meet the constraint. Picking it anyway ignores the constraint, inflating its score is dishonest, and deleting a real requirement hides a genuine need.

lessons.title: Pitfalls, 'Good Enough,' and Reversible Choices
lessons.content:
A decision matrix helps, but it does not think for you. This lesson covers the traps that fool good people and a few habits that keep your choices sane.

**Watch your biases.** One flashy feature can hijack a whole decision — letting a single number dominate everything else is called **anchoring**. Picking by gut alone, or chasing the **shiny object**, leads you to ignore what actually matters. Keep criteria **measurable**, not vague: 'lasts a full day on one charge' beats 'good battery.' And remember the matrix only reflects the weights you choose — **garbage weights give a garbage answer**. It supports your judgment; it never replaces it.

**Beware diminishing returns.** Pushing one quality to the extreme costs ever more for ever less gain. The last 5% of polish can cost as much as the first 95%. Often **'good enough' beats chasing perfect** — this is called **satisficing**, meaning you pick the first option that clears the bar instead of hunting forever for the ideal. Tie it back to requirements: **meet the requirement, then stop — do not gold-plate.** **Gold-plating** means adding cost and polish nobody asked for.

**Match effort to the stakes.** Some choices are easy to undo (**reversible**); some are hard or costly to undo (**irreversible**). When you are unsure, prefer cheap, reversible choices — you can change your mind. Renting before buying lets you test a neighborhood without being locked in. Save your deep analysis for the costly, hard-to-reverse decisions.

In short: name your criteria, weight what matters, compare honestly, respect hard constraints, avoid bias and gold-plating, and spend your effort where the choice is costly and hard to undo.

items.prompt: Letting one flashy feature dominate your whole decision is a bias called:
choices: Anchoring
Weighting
Prototyping
Iterating
items.explanation: Anchoring is when a single feature or number grabs too much attention and skews the whole choice. Weighting is the deliberate, balanced step of assigning importance; prototyping and iterating come from the design loop and are not biases.

items.prompt: Why is 'good battery' a weaker criterion than 'lasts a full day on one charge'?
choices: Because shorter phrases are always better
Because 'good battery' is vague, while a full day on one charge is measurable
Because battery life does not matter
Because vague criteria score higher automatically
items.explanation: Measurable criteria like 'a full day on one charge' can be checked and compared fairly, while 'good battery' is vague and means different things to different people. Phrase length is irrelevant, battery life clearly matters, and vague criteria do not earn higher scores; they just cause confusion.

items.prompt: What does the warning 'garbage weights give a garbage answer' mean for using a decision matrix?
choices: The matrix is useless and should never be used
The matrix is always right no matter what you enter
If your weights and scores are careless, the result is too; the matrix supports judgment but does not replace it
You should always give every criterion the same weight
items.explanation: The matrix only reflects the weights and scores you feed it, so poor inputs produce a poor answer; it organizes judgment rather than replacing it. It is not useless, it is not automatically right, and forcing equal weights would ignore that some criteria matter more.

items.prompt: What is 'diminishing returns'?
choices: The point where a product becomes free
Pushing a quality further costs more and more while adding less and less benefit
A rule that more effort always gives proportionally more quality
The idea that the first bit of effort is wasted
items.explanation: Diminishing returns means each extra push toward perfection costs more for smaller and smaller gains, like the last 5% of polish costing as much as the first 95%. It is not about things becoming free, effort does not always pay off proportionally, and early effort usually gives the biggest gains, not wasted ones.

items.prompt: Why can 'good enough' (satisficing) beat chasing the perfect option?
choices: Because perfect options are illegal
Because the extra cost of chasing perfect often buys very little real gain once you meet the requirement
Because quality never matters
Because the most expensive choice is always best
items.explanation: Once an option meets the requirement, pushing further hits diminishing returns, so the added cost buys little; picking the first option that clears the bar saves time and money. Perfect options are not illegal, quality does matter (the bar must still be met), and the priciest choice is not automatically best.

items.prompt: Adding extra cost and polish that nobody asked for, beyond what the requirement needs, is called:
choices: Satisficing
Meeting the requirement
Gold-plating
A hard constraint
items.explanation: Gold-plating is piling on cost and polish beyond the requirement, which wastes effort for little gain. Satisficing is the opposite (stopping once it is good enough), meeting the requirement is the goal itself, and a hard constraint is a must-have, not extra polish.

items.prompt: What is the difference between a reversible and an irreversible decision?
choices: A reversible decision is easy or cheap to undo; an irreversible one is hard or costly to undo
A reversible decision is always the wrong one
An irreversible decision costs nothing to change
There is no real difference between them
items.explanation: Reversible decisions can be undone cheaply, while irreversible ones are hard or expensive to take back, which is why they deserve more analysis. A reversible choice is not automatically wrong, irreversible choices are costly to change, and the difference is very real.

items.prompt: When you are unsure, why does renting a place before buying fit the 'reversible choice' idea?
choices: Because renting is always cheaper over a lifetime
Because renting lets you test the choice and change your mind, instead of locking in a costly hard-to-undo decision
Because buying can always be undone for free
Because reversibility means you should never commit to anything
items.explanation: Renting is a cheap, reversible way to test a neighborhood while uncertain, leaving room to change your mind, whereas buying is costly and hard to reverse. Renting is not always cheaper long-term, buying is not free to undo, and reversibility is about matching effort to stakes, not avoiding all commitment.

items.prompt: Which choice is the clearest example of a decision-making pitfall?
choices: Listing measurable criteria before comparing options
Buying the phone purely because one flashy feature caught your eye, ignoring price and battery
Checking hard constraints before scoring
Stopping once an option meets the requirement
items.explanation: Letting one shiny feature drive the whole purchase while ignoring what matters is the anchoring and shiny-object pitfall. Listing measurable criteria, checking constraints first, and stopping at good enough are all sound practices, not pitfalls.

lessons.title: Why Write Anything Down?
lessons.content:
Picture your favourite home-cooked meal. If the only person who knows how to make it never writes the recipe down, the dish disappears the day they forget it or are not around. Now write that recipe on a card, and anyone can recreate the meal, any time. That card is **documentation**: knowledge written down so it can be used again.

**Documentation** simply means recording information about something so others (and future-you) can build it, use it, or fix it without guessing. Flat-pack furniture is a great example: the assembly instructions let a total stranger build the shelf, even though they never met the designer.

Why bother? Because knowledge that lives only in one person's head is **fragile**. People forget details, get busy, or move on. When the know-how is only in a head, it can vanish. Written down, it becomes repeatable (anyone can follow it), shareable (you can hand it to someone else), and survivable (it outlasts any one person).

There is a handy way to think about it: **documentation is a letter to your future self**. Six months from now you will not remember why you did something or how it worked. A few clear notes save your future self hours of re-figuring-it-out.

So the first habit is simple: when something matters, write it down clearly. The rest of this sub-topic is about *what* to write and *how* to make it genuinely useful.

items.prompt: What does 'documentation' mean, in plain terms?
choices: Optional busywork you do only if there is spare time at the end
A drawing of how the finished product should look
The list of people who worked on a project
Recording information so others, and future-you, can build, use, or fix something
items.explanation: Documentation is knowledge written down so it can be reused, like a recipe or assembly instructions. Treating it as optional busywork is the classic trap; documentation is what makes work repeatable. A drawing or a list of people is not the same as recorded know-how for building, using, or fixing something.

items.prompt: Why is knowledge that lives only in one person's head described as 'fragile'?
choices: Because that person is probably not very smart
Because heads can only hold a small amount of information
Because people forget, get busy, or move on, and then the knowledge can vanish
Because spoken knowledge is always wrong
items.explanation: Head-only knowledge is fragile because the single person can forget details, be unavailable, or leave, taking the know-how with them. It has nothing to do with how smart they are or how much a head can hold, and spoken knowledge is not automatically wrong. Writing it down is what makes it survive.

items.prompt: A friend writes down their family recipe so anyone can recreate the dish later. Which benefit of documentation does this best show?
choices: It makes the knowledge repeatable and shareable, not stuck in one head
It proves the recipe is the tastiest in the world
It guarantees nobody else will ever cook that dish
It means the recipe never needs to be updated
items.explanation: Writing the recipe down lets anyone follow it (repeatable) and lets it be handed to others (shareable), so it no longer depends on one person's memory. It does not prove the dish is the best, it does not lock others out, and recipes can still need updating. The point is reuse beyond the original cook.

items.prompt: What does the phrase 'documentation is a letter to your future self' mean?
choices: You should mail your notes to yourself in the post
Notes are only ever useful to the person who wrote them
Clear notes now save future-you from re-figuring-out what you already knew
You should write documentation only after you have forgotten everything
items.explanation: The phrase means that months later you will have forgotten the details, so clear notes written now help your future self pick the work back up quickly. It is not about literal mail, and it is a misconception that docs are only for the writer; others read them too. You write while you still remember, not after forgetting.

items.prompt: Flat-pack furniture comes with assembly instructions. Why are those instructions a good example of documentation?
choices: They make the furniture more expensive to produce
They let a stranger who never met the designer build the furniture correctly
They are only useful to the person who designed the furniture
They replace the need for any physical parts
items.explanation: Good documentation lets someone with no prior knowledge carry out the work, exactly what assembly instructions do for a stranger building the shelf. They are written for the user, not just the designer, they do not raise production cost in any meaningful sense, and they certainly do not replace the parts. The value is transferring know-how to whoever needs it.

items.prompt: Someone says, 'I never write anything down; I just write docs for myself anyway, and I remember everything.' What is the best response?
choices: They are right; if you remember it, no one else needs it written down
They are right; writing things down is busywork that wastes time
Memory is always more reliable than any written note
Docs are mainly for others too, so written knowledge survives and can be shared, not just remembered
items.explanation: Documentation is not only for the author; it lets others build and maintain the work and survives even if you forget or leave. Believing docs are just for yourself, or that they are busywork, are the misconceptions here. Human memory is not more reliable than a clear written record, especially over time.

items.prompt: Which situation shows the value of writing things down most clearly?
choices: A team relies on one person who is the only one who knows how the system works
A new helper builds a bookshelf perfectly using the printed instructions, never having seen one before
Everyone keeps the steps only in their memory to save paper
A recipe is deliberately kept secret so the dish cannot be recreated
items.explanation: The new helper succeeding from written instructions shows documentation making work repeatable and shareable. Relying on one person, keeping steps only in memory, and hiding the recipe all do the opposite, leaving the knowledge fragile or unusable by others. Written-down know-how is what lets a newcomer act without guessing.

items.prompt: Good documentation makes work 'repeatable, shareable, and survivable.' What does 'survivable' mean here?
choices: The knowledge outlasts any one person, so it is not lost if someone leaves or forgets
The product can survive being dropped
The documentation can never be edited again
Only the original author can ever use it safely
items.explanation: Survivable means the know-how lives on beyond any single individual, so the work is not lost when a person forgets or moves on. It is about the knowledge, not the product's physical toughness. Documentation can still be edited, and it is meant for many readers, not just the author.

items.prompt: You finish a tricky task today. Why might you jot down a few clear notes even if no one else is involved?
choices: Because future-you will likely forget the details and the notes save re-figuring it out
Because notes make the task automatically run faster next time without you
Because writing notes is required before any task can be called finished
Because notes are only worth writing when a whole team will read them
items.explanation: Even solo, documentation is a letter to your future self: months later you will not recall the details, and notes spare you the rework. Notes do not magically run the task for you, they are not a universal rule for 'finished', and they are valuable even for an audience of one (your future self).

lessons.title: Specs and the Different Kinds of Docs
lessons.content:
You already know a **requirement** is a clear, testable statement of what is needed, like "a sturdy table." But "sturdy" alone does not tell a builder the exact size, materials, or how much weight it must hold. That precise, agreed description is a **specification**, or **spec** for short.

A **spec** is a precise description of *what* something is and does, with its agreed details: dimensions, behaviour, limits, and **interfaces** (the points where it connects to other things). It is the contract everyone builds and checks against. So requirements are the *needs*; the spec is the agreed, detailed description that satisfies them. For the table, the requirement is "sturdy"; the spec is "120 by 75 cm, oak top, four steel legs, holds 80 kg."

A spec is just one kind of document. Others include a **design doc**, which explains *how* something works and *why* choices were made; **user docs** (a manual or guide) that tell people *how to use* it; and a **maintenance** (or operations) doc that explains how to *fix or run* it. Two short ones are common too: a **README**, the quick "start here" overview, and a **change log**, a list of what changed and when.

The big idea is **audience-appropriate** writing: write for whoever reads it. A user manual speaks to a customer in plain steps; an internal design note speaks to a builder about trade-offs. Mixing them up, like handing a customer your internal design notes, leaves the reader lost. Match the document to its reader and its job.

items.prompt: What is a 'specification' (spec)?
choices: A rough wish for what someone hopes to get
A precise, agreed description of what something is and does, with its details and limits
A list of everyone who will use the product
A guess about how long the work will take
items.explanation: A spec is the precise, agreed description, dimensions, behaviour, limits, and interfaces, that everyone builds and checks against. A rough wish is closer to a vague requirement, not a spec. A user list and a time guess are different things entirely; neither pins down what the thing must be.

items.prompt: How do a requirement and a spec differ?
choices: They are exactly the same thing with two names
A requirement is detailed; a spec is always vague
A spec comes first, then you write the requirement from it
A requirement is the need; a spec is the agreed detailed description that satisfies it
items.explanation: Requirements state the needs (for example, 'a sturdy table'), while the spec gives the agreed precise details (exact size, materials, load) that meet those needs. They are not identical, the spec is the detailed one (not the vague one), and needs normally come before the detailed description, not after.

items.prompt: For a table, which of these is the SPEC rather than the requirement?
choices: "We need a sturdy table"
"It should feel nice and look good"
"120 by 75 cm, oak top, four steel legs, holds 80 kg"
"Something to put our dinner plates on"
items.explanation: The exact measurements, materials, and load limit are the agreed precise description, which is the spec. 'Sturdy', 'feel nice', and 'something for plates' are needs or wishes, the requirement side, not the detailed contract a builder works from.

items.prompt: Why is a spec sometimes called 'the contract everyone builds and checks against'?
choices: Because it is a legal document signed by lawyers
Because it sets the agreed details, so builders make to it and reviewers check the result against it
Because it lists the price each person must pay
Because it can be changed by anyone at any time without telling others
items.explanation: A spec works like a contract: it states the agreed details, so the people building aim for them and the people checking compare the result to them. It need not be a lawyer-signed document, it is not a price list, and a shared agreement is not something one person quietly rewrites at will.

items.prompt: Which document's job is to explain HOW something works and WHY the choices were made?
choices: A user manual
A change log
A design doc
A README
items.explanation: A design doc captures how the thing works and the reasoning behind decisions, aimed at people building or maintaining it. A user manual explains how to use it, a change log records what changed and when, and a README is the short 'start here' overview; none of those focus on the how-and-why of the design.

items.prompt: A customer just wants to know how to set up and use a product they bought. Which document is written for them?
choices: The internal design doc full of trade-off reasoning
The change log listing internal edits by date
The maintenance doc for repair technicians
The user manual (a guide in plain steps for using it)
items.explanation: User docs, like a manual, are written for the person using the product, in plain how-to steps. The design doc targets builders, the change log targets people tracking edits, and the maintenance doc targets repairers; handing a customer any of those would leave them lost. Match the doc to its reader.

items.prompt: What is a README, in plain terms?
choices: A detailed legal contract for the whole project
The quick 'start here' overview that orients a newcomer
A full repair manual for technicians
A list of everyone who paid for the project
items.explanation: A README is the short overview that tells a newcomer what something is and how to get started, the 'start here' note. It is not a legal contract, not a deep repair manual, and not a payer list. Its whole point is a fast, friendly orientation before the heavier docs.

items.prompt: What does it mean to write documentation that is 'audience-appropriate'?
choices: Write everything in the most technical language possible so it looks expert
Write one identical document and give it to absolutely everyone
Write for whoever will read it, so a user manual and an internal design note differ in style and detail
Write only for yourself, since you understand it best
items.explanation: Audience-appropriate means tailoring the document to its reader: a customer needs plain steps, a builder needs trade-off detail. Forcing maximum jargon, using one identical doc for everyone, or writing only for yourself all ignore who actually needs to act on it. The reader determines the right level and tone.

items.prompt: A change log and a maintenance doc are both useful. What does the change log specifically record?
choices: Step-by-step instructions for first-time users
What changed and when, over time
The precise agreed dimensions and materials
The reasons behind the original design choices
items.explanation: A change log lists what changed and when, giving a running history of updates. First-time use steps belong in the user manual, agreed dimensions and materials belong in the spec, and the reasoning behind choices belongs in the design doc. Each document carries a different, specific kind of information.

lessons.title: What Makes Docs Good (and What Bad Docs Cost)
lessons.content:
Having a document is not enough; it has to be a *good* one. Good documentation shares a few qualities. It is **clear and unambiguous** (only one sensible reading), **audience-appropriate** (pitched at whoever reads it), **accurate and up to date**, **findable** (organised so people can locate it), and **complete enough to act on without guessing**. Diagrams, examples, and defined terms all help.

The most dangerous failure is a **stale** doc, one that is out of date. A manual that describes last year's model can tell you to press the red button when the real product now uses a green one, causing a mistake. That is why people say a stale doc is **worse than none**: no doc makes you check carefully, but a wrong doc confidently misleads you.

The fix is a **single source of truth**: one authoritative copy that everyone refers to, instead of five conflicting drafts emailed around. We have all seen the chaos of "final_v2_FINAL_really.doc" where nobody knows which copy is correct. Because documents change, we also track **versions**, so it is clear which is the latest and what changed.

Why care so much? The **cost of bad or missing docs** is real: wasted time re-figuring things out, mistakes from misunderstanding, and the **bus factor**, the risk that if the one person who knows everything is suddenly gone, the work grinds to a halt. To wrap up: specs say precisely what to build; documentation makes it usable and maintainable; and good docs are clear, current, audience-aware, and kept as a single source of truth.

items.prompt: Which set of qualities best describes GOOD documentation?
choices: Long, full of jargon, and impressive-looking
Clear, audience-appropriate, accurate and up to date, findable, and complete enough to act on
Written once and never touched again
Kept private so only the author can read it
