items.explanation: Good docs are clear, pitched to their reader, current, easy to find, and complete enough to use without guessing. Length and jargon do not make docs good, never updating them invites staleness, and keeping them private defeats the point of sharing knowledge.

items.prompt: Why is a stale (out-of-date) document often called 'worse than no document at all'?
choices: Because it takes up storage space
Because old documents are always longer than new ones
Because it can only be read by the original author
Because it confidently misleads you into doing the wrong thing, while no doc makes you check carefully
items.explanation: A stale doc looks trustworthy but describes something that is no longer true, so it actively misleads, whereas having no doc at least prompts you to verify. The danger is being confidently wrong, not storage space or length, and readership is not the issue. Out-of-date guidance causes mistakes precisely because people trust it.

items.prompt: A manual says 'press the red button to stop', but the actual product now has a green stop button. What problem is this?
choices: The manual is too short
The manual is stale: it no longer matches the real product, so it can cause a mistake
The manual is using the wrong language
There is nothing wrong; manuals never need to match the product
items.explanation: This is a stale doc: it describes an older version, so a reader following it presses the wrong control and errs. Length and language are not the issue, and it is a misconception that a manual need not match the product; an accurate, up-to-date manual is exactly what prevents this mistake.

items.prompt: What is a 'single source of truth' for documentation?
choices: The oldest copy of the document, kept for history
A copy stored on every person's own computer
One authoritative copy that everyone refers to, instead of many conflicting copies
A document that no one is allowed to change
items.explanation: A single source of truth is the one official copy everyone uses, so there is no confusion about which version is correct. The oldest copy is not authoritative, scattering copies onto everyone's computer creates the very conflict you want to avoid, and the source of truth is updated over time, not frozen.

items.prompt: A team emails around files named 'plan_final.doc', 'plan_final_v2.doc', and 'plan_v2_FINAL_really.doc'. Which idea fixes this mess?
choices: Keep even more copies, since more backups are always safer
Keep one shared, versioned document as the single source of truth
Delete all documentation so there is nothing to conflict
Let each person trust whichever copy they happen to have
items.explanation: One shared, versioned copy that everyone refers to ends the confusion over which file is correct. Keeping more conflicting copies makes it worse, not safer; deleting all docs throws away needed knowledge; and trusting whatever copy you hold is exactly how mistakes creep in. The cure is a single source of truth with tracked versions.

items.prompt: Why do we track 'versions' of a document?
choices: To make the document look more official
Because documents change, so versions show which copy is the latest and what changed
To stop anyone from ever editing the document
Because every reader needs their own private edition
items.explanation: Versioning records that documents evolve, making it clear which copy is current and what was changed. It is not about appearances, it does not freeze the document (it embraces change in an orderly way), and the goal is one shared history, not a private edition per reader. Versions keep a changing doc trustworthy.

items.prompt: What does the 'bus factor' describe?
choices: How many people can fit on a project team
How fast a project can travel from start to finish
The cost of commuting to the office
The risk that if the one person who knows everything is suddenly gone, the work grinds to a halt
items.explanation: Bus factor is the danger of depending on a single person whose knowledge is not written down; if they leave or are unavailable, progress stops. It is not about team size, project speed, or commuting. Documentation lowers the bus factor by spreading knowledge so no single absence is fatal.

items.prompt: You inherit a project with no notes, no README, and no spec. What is the most likely cost of these missing docs?
choices: The project will automatically run faster without paperwork
Wasted time re-figuring things out and mistakes from misunderstanding
Nothing; missing docs never cause problems
The project becomes cheaper because no one wrote anything
items.explanation: Missing documentation forces you to reconstruct knowledge from scratch and invites errors from guessing, the real cost of bad or no docs. It does not speed things up or save money in any genuine sense, and the idea that missing docs are harmless is the misconception this lesson warns against. Inheriting a no-notes project is a classic painful example.

items.prompt: Pulling it together: which statement best summarises the role of specs and documentation?
choices: Specs and docs are optional extras you add only if a customer demands them
A spec and a user manual are the same document with different covers
Specs say precisely what to build; documentation makes it usable and maintainable, and good docs stay clear, current, audience-aware, and a single source of truth
Keeping several conflicting copies is safer than maintaining one authoritative version
items.explanation: The spec pins down precisely what to build, while documentation (design, user, maintenance, README, change log) makes it usable and maintainable, and good docs are clear, current, audience-appropriate, and kept as one source of truth. Treating docs as optional, confusing a spec with a user manual, and preferring many conflicting copies are all misconceptions the sub-topic set out to correct.

lessons.title: Accuracy vs Precision: Why Good Measuring Matters
lessons.content:
Every decision you make rests on the numbers behind it. If your bathroom scale or your kitchen timer quietly lies to you, every choice built on that reading is shaky too. So before we touch any instrument, the first habit is simple: **a decision is only as good as the measurement behind it.** Measuring well is a skill you can learn.

The foundation is one distinction people constantly mix up: **accuracy** versus **precision**. **Accuracy** is how close your reading is to the *true* value (the real, correct amount). **Precision** is how *consistent* your readings are with each other when you repeat them, whether or not they are correct. They are not the same thing.

The classic picture is throwing darts at a board. Three cases tell the whole story. (1) Darts in a tight little cluster, but off to one side, away from the bullseye: that is **precise but not accurate**, consistent yet consistently wrong. (2) Darts spread out all around the bullseye, none tight together but centered on it on average: that is **accurate-ish but not precise**, right in the middle overall yet scattered. (3) Darts in a tight cluster right on the bullseye: that is **both accurate and precise**, the goal.

The trap to avoid is assuming a steady, repeatable reading must be correct. You can be perfectly consistent and perfectly wrong at the same time. Recap: accuracy means close to the truth, precision means close to each other, and the rest of this sub-topic is about getting both.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is the difference between accuracy and precision?
choices: Accuracy is how close to the true value; precision is how consistent your readings are with each other
Accuracy and precision mean the same thing
Accuracy is how many decimal places you show; precision is how fast you measure
Precision is how close to the true value; accuracy is how expensive the instrument is
items.explanation: Accuracy is closeness to the real, true value; precision is how repeatable or consistent your readings are with one another, regardless of whether they are correct. They are not synonyms, and neither one is about decimal places, speed, or price. Keeping the two ideas separate is the whole foundation of measuring well.

items.prompt: Darts land in a tight little cluster, but well off to the side of the bullseye. Which case is this?
choices: Precise but not accurate
Accurate but not precise
Both accurate and precise
Neither accurate nor precise
items.explanation: A tight cluster means the throws are consistent with each other, so they are precise. But being off to the side means they are not close to the true target, so they are not accurate. This is the classic 'consistently wrong' case, the easiest one to mistake for good measuring.

items.prompt: Darts are scattered all around the bullseye, none close together, but centered on it on average. Which case is this?
choices: Precise but not accurate
Both accurate and precise
Neither accurate nor precise
Accurate on average but not precise
items.explanation: Spread-out throws are not consistent with each other, so they are not precise. But because they center on the bullseye overall, on average they are accurate-ish. People often flip this with the tight-but-off case; the key is that scatter is a precision problem, while being off-center is an accuracy problem.

items.prompt: A kitchen scale gives the exact same reading three times in a row for the same bowl. Can you conclude the reading is correct?
choices: Yes, repeating the same number always proves it is the true value
Yes, but only if the scale is digital
No, it could be precise but consistently wrong; repeatable does not mean correct
No, because a scale can never be trusted at all
items.explanation: Getting the same number every time shows the scale is precise (consistent), but it says nothing about accuracy. The scale could be steadily off, giving you the same wrong value each time. Believing a repeatable reading must be true is exactly the misconception this lesson warns against; consistency and correctness are separate.

items.prompt: Why is it said that 'a decision is only as good as the measurement behind it'?
choices: Because measurements are just a formality and rarely affect decisions
Because the slowest measurement is always the most trustworthy
Because decisions should be made without any measurements
Because if the underlying reading is wrong, any choice built on it is shaky too
items.explanation: Decisions rest on the numbers feeding them, so a faulty reading quietly undermines every choice made from it. Measurements are not a mere formality; they are the foundation. Slowness does not guarantee trust, and making decisions with no measurement at all is worse, not better.

items.prompt: Darts land in a tight cluster right on the bullseye. How would you describe this?
choices: Precise but not accurate
Accurate but not precise
Both accurate and precise, which is the goal
Neither accurate nor precise
items.explanation: Tight together means precise, and centered on the bullseye means accurate, so this is both at once, the target you want from good measuring. The other options each describe a problem (off-center, scattered, or both) that this picture does not have.

items.prompt: Two stopwatches both time the same 10-second event. One reads 10.0 s every time; the other reads values jumping between 8 s and 12 s. What can you say?
choices: The first is more precise (consistent); the second is scattered, so less precise
The second is more precise because its numbers are bigger and smaller
Both are equally precise because they average the same
Precision cannot be judged from repeated readings
items.explanation: Precision is about how consistent repeated readings are. The first stopwatch repeats the same value, so it is precise; the second jumps around, so it is not. Whether either is accurate (truly 10 s) is a separate question; here we are only judging consistency, which is exactly what repeated readings reveal.

items.prompt: Someone insists, 'My measurement shows more decimal places, so it must be the most accurate.' What is the problem with this reasoning?
choices: Nothing; more decimal places always means more accuracy
Showing more digits is about apparent detail, not about being close to the true value
More decimal places make a reading less precise
Decimal places only matter for time, not weight
items.explanation: Extra decimal places only add apparent detail; they do not make a reading any closer to the true value. A scale can display 4.0000 kg and still be badly off if it is miscalibrated. Confusing 'more digits' with 'more accurate' is a common beginner trap; accuracy is about truth, not the number of digits shown.

items.prompt: You weigh the same letter on two scales. Scale A reads 20 g, 20 g, 20 g. Scale B reads 18 g, 22 g, 20 g. The true weight is 20 g. Which statement is correct?
choices: Scale A is both accurate and precise here; Scale B is accurate on average but less precise
Scale B is more precise because its readings vary
Scale A must be wrong because real readings always vary
Neither scale can be accurate because they disagree with each other
items.explanation: Scale A repeats 20 g (precise) and 20 g is the true value (accurate), so it is both. Scale B averages 20 g (accurate on average) but its readings scatter (less precise). Varying readings are not a sign of precision, a steady true reading is not automatically suspicious, and the scales disagreeing does not by itself rule out accuracy.

lessons.title: Calibration, Resolution, and Range
lessons.content:
Instruments are not born perfect and they do not stay perfect. Over time they **drift** (slowly change) and they can carry an **offset** (a steady amount they are off by). A bathroom scale that shows 2 kg with nothing on it has an offset: every reading will be 2 kg too high until you fix it.

The fix is **calibration**: checking, and if needed adjusting, an instrument against a **known reference** so its readings can be trusted. When you press 'zero' or 'tare' on a kitchen scale before weighing flour, that is everyday calibration, you are telling it 'empty means zero'. For accuracy you compare against a reference of known value, like a weight stamped 1.000 kg. That reference is itself checked against a more official one; this chain back to an agreed standard is called being **traceable to a standard**. The reason all this matters: an uncalibrated instrument does not refuse to answer, it gives you a confident wrong number.

Two more limits every tool has. **Resolution** is the smallest change an instrument can actually show. A ruler marked only in millimetres cannot reliably read tenths of a millimetre, and a scale that shows only whole grams cannot tell 4 g from 4.4 g. **Range** is the span it can measure at all: a kitchen scale maxes out and a thermometer has a top and bottom limit. The golden rule: never claim more precision than the tool's resolution allows. If the smallest mark is 1 mm, do not report 1.547 cm. Recap: calibrate against a trusted reference to fix offsets and drift, and respect resolution and range so you never claim detail the instrument cannot deliver.

items.prompt: What is calibration, in plain terms?
choices: Checking, and if needed adjusting, an instrument against a known reference so its readings are trustworthy
Cleaning an instrument so it looks new
Reading an instrument as fast as possible
Buying a more expensive instrument
items.explanation: Calibration means comparing an instrument to a trusted reference of known value and adjusting it so its readings can be believed. It is not about cleaning, speed, or price. Without it, an instrument can be confidently wrong, which is exactly what calibration guards against.

items.prompt: A bathroom scale reads 2 kg when nothing is on it. What does this show, and what is the right response?
choices: It shows the scale is broken beyond use; throw it away
It shows an offset; the scale needs zeroing or calibrating so it reads 0 when empty
It shows the scale is very precise, so trust it as is
It shows nothing important; just subtract nothing and carry on
items.explanation: Reading 2 kg with nothing on it is a classic offset: every reading is shifted by 2 kg. The fix is to zero or calibrate the scale against a known reference so empty reads zero. An offset does not mean the scale is junk, and it certainly is not a sign to trust the readings as they are.

items.prompt: Pressing 'tare' (zero) on a kitchen scale before adding flour is an everyday example of what?
choices: Increasing the scale's range
Calibration: telling the instrument that empty means zero so its readings start from a known point
Improving the scale's resolution
A pointless step that changes nothing
items.explanation: Taring sets a known reference point (empty equals zero) so the reading you get is just the flour, a small act of everyday calibration. It does not change how much the scale can hold (range) or the smallest step it can show (resolution), and it is far from pointless; it removes the weight of the bowl from your reading.

items.prompt: What does it mean for a reference weight to be 'traceable to a standard'?
choices: The weight can be tracked by GPS
The weight was bought from an official-looking shop
The reference is itself checked against a more official standard, forming a chain back to an agreed one
The weight is heavier than any other weight
items.explanation: Traceable means your reference has itself been compared to a more authoritative standard, which links back through a chain to an official agreed standard. That chain is what lets you trust the reference. It has nothing to do with GPS tracking, where it was purchased, or how heavy it is.

items.prompt: Someone says, 'A good instrument never needs calibration once you buy it.' Why is this wrong?
choices: It is not wrong; quality instruments stay perfect forever
Because instruments drift over time and can carry offsets, so they need checking against a reference
Because calibration makes instruments worse
Because only cheap instruments can ever be calibrated
items.explanation: Even good instruments drift slowly and can develop offsets, so periodic checking against a known reference is what keeps them trustworthy. Believing an instrument never needs calibration is a common misconception. Calibration improves readings rather than harming them, and it applies to quality instruments just as much as cheap ones.

items.prompt: What does the 'resolution' of an instrument mean?
choices: The largest amount it can measure before maxing out
How close its readings are to the true value
How quickly it gives an answer
The smallest change it can actually show
items.explanation: Resolution is the smallest change an instrument can display, such as 1 mm on a millimetre ruler or 1 g on a whole-gram scale. The largest measurable amount is the range, closeness to truth is accuracy, and quickness is speed. Resolution sets the finest detail you can honestly read.

items.prompt: A ruler is marked only in millimetres. You measure an edge that falls between the 15 mm and 16 mm marks. What is the honest way to report it?
choices: Report it as exactly 15.473 mm for extra detail
Report it as about 15 to 16 mm; you cannot reliably claim tenths the ruler does not show
Report it as 100 mm to be safe
Refuse to report anything because the ruler is useless
items.explanation: The smallest division is 1 mm, so the most you can honestly say is that the edge lies between 15 and 16 mm. Claiming 15.473 mm invents precision the tool cannot deliver, the misconception to avoid. The ruler is not useless, and inflating to 100 mm is simply wrong; respect the resolution limit.

items.prompt: What does the 'range' of an instrument mean?
choices: The smallest change it can show
How close it is to the true value
The span of values it can measure at all, with a lowest and highest limit
The number of decimal places on its display
items.explanation: Range is the full span an instrument can measure, from its lowest to its highest limit, like a kitchen scale that maxes out or a thermometer with top and bottom bounds. The smallest readable step is resolution, closeness to truth is accuracy, and decimal places are just display detail.

items.prompt: A kitchen scale shows only whole grams. A recipe needs 4.4 g of yeast. What is the real limitation here?
choices: The scale's range is too small for 4.4 g
The scale's resolution (whole grams) cannot distinguish 4.4 g from 4 g, so it cannot show that detail
The scale is uncalibrated and reads an offset
The scale measures too quickly to be accurate
items.explanation: Showing only whole grams is a resolution limit: the scale steps in 1 g jumps and cannot tell 4.4 g from 4 g. This is not about range (4.4 g is well within what it can hold) or an offset, and speed is irrelevant. Knowing your resolution stops you from expecting detail the tool cannot provide.

lessons.title: Reading Instruments Right, and Two Kinds of Error
lessons.content:
Even a calibrated tool with fine resolution can mislead you if you use it carelessly. Good practice is a handful of habits. Use the **right tool for the precision you need**. Let an instrument **settle or warm up** before trusting it, a thermometer needs a moment to reach the real temperature. Read it **straight-on** to avoid **parallax**, the small error you get when your eye is not square to the scale or needle, so the mark looks shifted; reading a measuring cup at eye level fixes this. Try **not to disturb what you measure**: a cold thermometer dropped into a tiny sample actually cools that sample, changing the very thing you wanted, a plain-language version of the 'observer effect'. Finally, **take several readings** and watch for an **outlier** (a stray value that does not fit), and always **record your units and conditions**.

This leads to two kinds of error worth naming. A **systematic error** is a consistent bias in one direction, like the scale that always reads 2 kg high. Because it is steady, averaging more readings will not remove it; you fix it by **calibrating** against a reference. A **random error** is the harmless jitter that makes single readings wobble a little above and below the truth. You reduce random error by taking several readings and **averaging**, the scatter tends to cancel out.

So: bias you calibrate out, scatter you average down, they are different problems with different fixes. We have kept the numbers light on purpose. How to write a result with its uncertainty, and how many digits you are really allowed to keep (significant figures), is the focus of the next sub-topic.

items.prompt: What is parallax, and how do you avoid it?
choices: A type of calibration; avoid it by buying a reference weight
The largest value an instrument can show; avoid it by using a bigger instrument
The reading error from viewing a scale at an angle; avoid it by reading straight-on, eye square to the scale
Random jitter in readings; avoid it by averaging
items.explanation: Parallax is the small error that appears when your eye is off to the side, so the mark or needle looks shifted; reading straight-on, with your eye square to the scale, removes it. It is not calibration, not the instrument's range, and not random jitter. Reading a measuring cup at eye level is the everyday fix.

items.prompt: You read a measuring cup from above, looking down at an angle, instead of at eye level. What problem are you risking?
choices: A parallax error, because your eye is not square to the scale
A resolution error, because the cup has too few marks
An offset that calibration would fix
No problem at all; viewing angle never matters
items.explanation: Looking at the scale from an angle is the textbook cause of parallax: the liquid level appears to line up with the wrong mark. The cure is to read at eye level, square to the scale. This is not about how many marks the cup has (resolution) or a calibration offset, and viewing angle definitely does matter.

items.prompt: What is the difference between systematic error and random error?
choices: Systematic error is a consistent bias in one direction; random error is harmless scatter above and below the truth
They are the same thing with different names
Systematic error is scatter; random error is a steady offset
Systematic error only happens with digital tools; random error only with analog ones
items.explanation: Systematic error is a steady bias, like a scale always reading high, while random error is the small wobble that scatters readings above and below the true value. They are different problems, not the same, and the definitions are not swapped: bias is the consistent one, scatter is the random one. Neither is tied to digital versus analog.

items.prompt: A scale always reads 2 kg too high, every single time. Will taking many readings and averaging fix this?
choices: Yes, averaging removes any error, including a steady offset
Yes, but only if you take exactly ten readings
No; nothing can ever fix a scale with an offset
No; a consistent bias stays after averaging, so you must calibrate it out against a reference
items.explanation: A steady 2 kg overshoot is a systematic error, the same in every reading, so averaging just gives you the same biased result. The fix is calibration against a known reference. Averaging only helps with random scatter, not a consistent bias, and the offset is certainly fixable by calibrating.

items.prompt: Your stopwatch readings of the same event jitter a little: 9.9 s, 10.1 s, 10.0 s, 9.8 s, 10.2 s. What is the best way to get a steadier value?
choices: Take several readings and average them, since random scatter tends to cancel out
Keep only the first reading and ignore the rest
Calibrate the stopwatch, because this is a fixed offset
Add more decimal places to each reading
items.explanation: Readings that wobble above and below a central value show random error, which averaging several readings reduces because the scatter cancels. A single reading throws away that benefit. This is not a fixed offset, so calibration is not the tool here, and extra decimal places add no real information.

items.prompt: You dip a cold thermometer into a single spoonful of warm water and read it instantly. Why might the reading be off?
choices: The thermometer has too much resolution
The cold thermometer cools the small sample and changes its temperature, and it had no time to settle
Reading it instantly always gives the most accurate value
Small samples are always hotter than they appear
items.explanation: A cold thermometer pulls heat from a tiny sample, lowering the very temperature you are trying to measure, the everyday 'observer effect', and it also needs a moment to settle to the true value. Reading instantly is the opposite of good practice here. The issue is not resolution, and small samples are not inherently hotter.

items.prompt: Which of these is a good measurement habit?
choices: Trust a single reading and never repeat it, to save time
Always use whatever tool is nearest, regardless of the precision needed
Let the instrument settle, read straight-on, take several readings, and record the units and conditions
Report as many decimal places as possible no matter the tool
items.explanation: Good practice is to let the instrument settle, read it straight-on to avoid parallax, repeat the reading to catch outliers, and record units and conditions. Trusting one reading, grabbing the wrong tool for the job, and inventing decimal places are all the habits this lesson warns against.

items.prompt: You take five readings and one is wildly different from the other four. What is the careful thing to do?
choices: Treat the stray value as an outlier worth a second look, rather than blindly averaging it in
Always keep the stray value because more data is always better
Throw away all five readings and give up
Assume the stray value is the only correct one
items.explanation: A value that does not fit the others is an outlier and deserves a closer look; it may be a slip or a disturbance, so you do not just average it in unexamined. Blindly keeping it can skew the result, discarding all the data is overkill, and assuming the odd one out is the truth has it backwards.

items.prompt: Pulling it together: which statement best summarises measuring well?
choices: More decimal places mean more accuracy, and one reading is always enough

Know accuracy vs precision, calibrate against a reference, respect resolution and range, read carefully (parallax, settling, do not disturb), and repeat readings; calibrate out bias, average down scatter
A repeatable reading is always correct, so calibration is never needed
Pick any tool and trust its first reading without checking units
items.explanation: Trustworthy data comes from understanding accuracy versus precision, calibrating against a reference, respecting resolution and range, reading instruments carefully, and repeating measurements, with systematic bias calibrated out and random scatter averaged down. The other options repeat the very misconceptions covered here: more digits is not more accuracy, a repeatable reading is not automatically correct, and one unchecked reading is not enough. Writing results with uncertainty and significant figures comes next.

lessons.title: Always Carry Units (and Convert Them Cleanly)
lessons.content:
The last sub-topic was about measuring honestly. This one is about the **numbers that come out of measuring** and how to handle them without fooling yourself. The very first habit, the cardinal rule, is simple: **a number on its own is meaningless.** If a recipe just says '2', you have to ask: 2 what? Cups? Teaspoons? The number 2 alone cannot answer. The fix is to always attach a **unit** (the kind of thing being counted, like meters, grams, or seconds). '5' tells you almost nothing; '5 meters' tells you a length everyone agrees on.

This is not fussiness. In 1999 a Mars spacecraft was lost because one team worked in metric units and another in imperial units, and the mismatched numbers were never reconciled. A missing unit is not just untidy, it can be dangerous.

Once you carry units, you can **convert** between them with one clean trick: multiply by a fraction that equals 1. Since 1 km = 1000 m, the fraction (1000 m / 1 km) equals 1, and multiplying by 1 never changes the real amount, only how it is written. So 3 km becomes 3 km × (1000 m / 1 km) = 3000 m; the 'km' on top and bottom cancel, leaving meters. The same works for time: 1 hour = 60 minutes, so 2 hours = 120 minutes.

This leads to a free error-checker called **dimensional analysis**: just check that the units on both sides match. A speed must come out as a distance over a time (like miles per hour). If your 'speed' calculation produced 'meters per kilogram', you made a mistake, no matter how tidy the arithmetic looked. Recap: never write a bare number, convert by multiplying by a fraction equal to 1, and let the units themselves catch your blunders.

items.prompt: Why is a number like '5' with no unit attached a problem?
choices: It is fine; numbers never need units
On its own it is meaningless, because you cannot tell '5 of what' (meters? feet? seconds?)
It is a problem only if the number is large
It just looks untidy, but it is always perfectly clear
items.explanation: A bare number does not say what it counts, so '5' could be meters, feet, seconds, or anything. Attaching a unit ('5 meters') is what makes it mean something everyone agrees on. This matters for any size of number, and it is far more than a tidiness issue, it is the cardinal rule of handling numbers honestly.

items.prompt: A recipe step simply says '2'. What is the safe thing to conclude?
choices: Use 2 of whatever is largest in the kitchen
The number is complete as written; just add 2
Nothing safely: without a unit (cups? teaspoons?), the '2' does not tell you the amount
Always assume it means 2 cups
items.explanation: '2' alone is ambiguous, 2 cups and 2 teaspoons are wildly different amounts, so you cannot safely act on it. The honest conclusion is that the unit is missing. Guessing 'largest item' or always assuming cups invents information; the number simply is not complete until it carries a unit.

items.prompt: What real lesson does the lost Mars spacecraft (metric vs imperial mix-up) teach?
choices: Units are just a formality with no real consequences
Spacecraft should never use numbers at all
Imperial units are always wrong
Mixing up units can cause real disasters, so numbers must always carry the right unit
items.explanation: Two teams used different units and never reconciled them, and the spacecraft was lost, a vivid reminder that units carry real, sometimes dangerous, consequences. It is not that one system is 'wrong'; the failure was the mismatch. The fix is always carrying and agreeing on units, not abandoning numbers.

items.prompt: You want to convert 3 km into meters. Using 1 km = 1000 m, what is the result?
choices: 3 m
items.explanation: Multiply by the fraction equal to 1: 3 km × (1000 m / 1 km). The 'km' cancels, and 3 × 1000 = 3000, so the answer is 3000 m. The other options drop or add zeros, which is exactly the slip the cancel-the-units method helps you avoid.

items.prompt: Why does multiplying 3 km by (1000 m / 1 km) NOT change the actual length?
choices: Because multiplying always shrinks a number
Because the fraction (1000 m / 1 km) equals 1, and multiplying by 1 changes only the unit, not the amount
Because kilometers and meters measure different things
Because you are allowed to ignore the bottom of the fraction
items.explanation: Since 1 km and 1000 m are the same distance, the fraction (1000 m / 1 km) is just 1 in disguise, and multiplying by 1 leaves the amount untouched while swapping the unit. Multiplying does not always shrink a number, km and m measure the same thing (length), and you may never ignore the bottom of the fraction.

items.prompt: Using 1 hour = 60 minutes, how many minutes are in 2 hours?
choices: 62 minutes
30 minutes
120 minutes
200 minutes
items.explanation: Multiply by the fraction equal to 1: 2 hours × (60 minutes / 1 hour) = 120 minutes. The 'hour' cancels and 2 × 60 = 120. Adding 60 (giving 62) or dividing instead of multiplying (giving 30) are common mix-ups; tracking the units keeps the operation straight.

items.prompt: What does 'dimensional analysis' mean, in plain terms?
choices: Measuring the physical dimensions (length and width) of an object
Checking that the units on both sides of a calculation match, as a way to catch mistakes
Adding more decimal places to a result
Converting every number into kilometers
items.explanation: Dimensional analysis is simply checking that the units line up: if a result comes out in the wrong kind of unit, the calculation is wrong. It is not about an object's physical size, not about decimal places, and not about forcing everything into one unit. It is a free error-checker that uses the units themselves.

items.prompt: You calculate a speed and your answer comes out in 'meters per kilogram'. What should you conclude?
choices: The answer is fine as long as the arithmetic was neat
Speed is sometimes measured in meters per kilogram
You should just relabel it as miles per hour
Something is wrong: a speed must be a distance over a time, not a distance over a weight
items.explanation: A speed must have the form distance-over-time (like miles per hour), so 'meters per kilogram' signals a real error, however tidy the numbers look. You cannot fix it by relabeling; the wrong units mean a wrong step somewhere. This is dimensional analysis catching a blunder for you.

items.prompt: A friend says 'the distance is 7, that's all you need to know.' What is the honest response?
choices: 7 needs a unit, 7 meters and 7 miles are very different, so '7' alone is not enough
Agree, since the number 7 fully describes the distance
Ask for more decimal places, like 7.0000
Assume 7 means 7 kilometers because that is most common
items.explanation: A distance of '7' is meaningless until you know the unit; 7 meters and 7 miles differ enormously. The honest move is to ask for the unit, not to accept the bare number, pile on decimal places (which add no real information), or guess a unit. Always carry the unit.

items.prompt: When you convert 3 km to 3000 m by multiplying by (1000 m / 1 km), why does the answer end up in meters?
choices: Because meters are always bigger than kilometers
Because you flipped the fraction upside down
Because the 'km' on top and the 'km' on the bottom cancel, leaving 'm' as the surviving unit
Because you are free to pick whichever unit you like for the answer
items.explanation: The starting 'km' cancels with the 'km' in the bottom of the fraction, so only the 'm' from the top survives, that is why the answer is in meters. Meters are actually smaller than kilometers, you did not flip the fraction, and you cannot just pick a unit; the cancellation decides it.

lessons.title: Significant Figures: Don't Fake Precision
lessons.content:
Imagine you measure a length with a tape and read **3.2 cm**. Could you then report it as 3.247 cm? No, those extra digits are made up, your tape never showed them. This is the idea behind **significant figures** (often shortened to 'sig figs'): the digits in a number that carry real, measured information. In 3.2 cm there are two significant figures; in the fictional 3.247 cm the last two digits are invented.

Why care? Because reporting more digits than you actually measured is a quiet form of dishonesty. Measuring a room with a tape to the nearest centimeter and then claiming you know it to the width of a hair (a micrometer) is simply not true. The number looks impressive, but it is fake.

A couple of plain rules keep you honest. First, the **weakest-link rule**: a result is only as precise as its least-precise input. Like a chain that is only as strong as its weakest link, if one measurement is rough, the answer is rough too. So when you **multiply or divide**, keep about as many significant figures as the least-precise value going in. Second, **round only at the very end.** If you round in the middle of a calculation and then keep computing, the early rounding error grows; carry the extra digits through and round once at the finish.

Here is one clean example. You measure a small rectangle as 3.2 cm by 2.0 cm (each has two sig figs). The area is 3.2 × 2.0 = 6.4 square cm. You report **6.4**, not 6.40000, because your inputs only justify two significant figures. The trailing zeros would be pretending to a precision you never had. Recap: keep only the digits your measurement supports, let the least-precise input set the limit, and round at the end, honest numbers beat impressive-looking fake ones.

items.prompt: What are 'significant figures'?
choices: The digits in a number that carry real, measured information
Only the digits that come after the decimal point
The largest digits in a number
Any digits a calculator happens to display
items.explanation: Significant figures are the digits that actually carry measured information, the ones your instrument truly supported. They are not just the digits after the decimal point, not the 'largest' digits, and certainly not whatever long string a calculator shows. The whole point is to distinguish real digits from made-up ones.

items.prompt: You measured a length as 3.2 cm with a tape. Can you honestly report it as 3.247 cm?
choices: Yes, more decimal places always make a measurement better
Yes, as long as you used a calculator
No: the tape never showed the .047, so those digits are made up
No, but only because 3.247 is hard to read
items.explanation: Your tape only resolved down to 3.2 cm, so the extra digits .047 were never measured, they are invented. Tacking on decimal places does not improve a measurement, and a calculator cannot create information the tape did not capture. Reporting 3.2 cm is the honest choice.

items.prompt: How many significant figures does the measurement 3.2 cm have?
choices: Four
Two
One
Zero
items.explanation: Both the 3 and the 2 are measured digits that carry real information, so 3.2 cm has two significant figures. There are not four (that would require two more measured digits you never had), and it is more than one, since the 2 after the decimal point is also a genuine reading.

items.prompt: You measure a rectangle as 3.2 cm by 2.0 cm (two sig figs each). How should you report the area?
choices: 6.40000 square cm, to look precise
6.4 square cm, keeping two significant figures
6 square cm, dropping the decimal entirely
6.400 square cm
items.explanation: The area is 3.2 × 2.0 = 6.4 square cm. Since each input has two significant figures, the answer should keep about two as well, so 6.4 is right. Writing 6.40000 or 6.400 fakes precision your measurements never had, while 6 throws away a digit you genuinely measured.

items.prompt: What is the 'weakest-link' rule for significant figures?
choices: A result is only as precise as its least-precise input, like a chain being only as strong as its weakest link
Always keep as many digits as your most precise input
The smallest number in a calculation should be ignored
You should always round to one significant figure
items.explanation: A calculation cannot be more precise than its roughest input, just as a chain breaks at its weakest link, so you keep about as many sig figs as the least-precise value. It is the least-precise, not the most-precise, input that sets the limit; you do not ignore the small number or blindly collapse everything to one digit.

items.prompt: Why should you round only at the very end of a calculation, not in the middle?
choices: Rounding early is fine and makes no difference
Because you are never allowed to round at all
Because the middle of a calculation has no numbers
Because rounding in the middle and then continuing lets that early error grow into the final answer
items.explanation: If you round partway through and keep computing, that small rounding error feeds forward and can grow, throwing off the result. So you carry extra digits through the steps and round once at the end. Rounding early is not harmless, and you certainly do round, just at the finish, not mid-stream.

items.prompt: You measure a room with a tape to the nearest centimeter, then claim you know its size to the width of a hair. What is wrong?
choices: You are claiming far more precision than the tape can give, which is fake precision
Nothing; a tape measures to the width of a hair
You should have used more decimal places to be safe
Rooms cannot be measured with a tape at all
items.explanation: A tape read to the nearest centimeter simply cannot resolve hair-width detail, so claiming that precision is inventing digits, the dishonesty sig figs guard against. Piling on decimal places makes it worse, not safer, and tapes absolutely can measure rooms; the error is overstating how finely.

items.prompt: A calculator shows 6.39999998 for an area built from two-sig-fig measurements. What should you report?
choices: All of the calculator's digits, since the calculator is exact
6.4 square cm, because your inputs only justify two significant figures
6.39999998 square cm, rounded to nowhere
As many digits as will fit on the page
items.explanation: The calculator's long string is not 'extra accuracy', it just reflects its internal arithmetic; your two-sig-fig inputs only support about two figures, so you report 6.4. Keeping all the digits is the classic trap of treating a calculator readout as truth, when the data behind it is far rougher.

items.prompt: You divide a measurement known to two sig figs by one known to four sig figs. About how many sig figs should the answer have?
choices: Four, matching the more precise input
Six, by adding the two counts together
About two, matching the least-precise input
As many as the calculator shows
items.explanation: By the weakest-link rule, the least-precise input (two sig figs) sets the limit, so the answer should carry about two. You do not match the more precise value, and you never add the counts together. Reporting the calculator's full readout would again be faking precision the data cannot support.

lessons.title: Uncertainty and the Sanity Check
lessons.content:
No measurement is perfectly exact, so every honest number comes with a **give-or-take**, written with a plus-or-minus sign. A bag of flour might be **100 g ± 2 g**, meaning the true weight is probably somewhere between 98 g and 102 g. This give-or-take is called the **uncertainty**, and reporting it is part of telling the truth about a number. It ties straight back to the last sub-topic: random scatter in repeated readings becomes a ± range, and averaging several readings shrinks that range.

What happens when you combine numbers? In plain words, **the answer is no more certain than its inputs, and combining noisy inputs gives a noisier result.** A useful concrete case: when you **add** quantities, their give-or-takes add up too. Two bags at 100 g ± 2 g each give 200 g, and the give-or-takes combine, so you would not claim the total is exact. (We are keeping this at the level of the idea, no formulas, just: errors propagate, and the result inherits the doubt of its parts.)

Finally, a habit that catches big blunders for free: the **sanity check**, also called an order-of-magnitude estimate. Before trusting an answer, ask, does its SIZE make sense? If a calculation says a car weighs 5 grams, or a cross-town trip takes 0.001 seconds, you have slipped a unit or a decimal point somewhere. A quick rough guess, often called a **Fermi estimate** (a back-of-envelope, common-sense approximation), gives you a ballpark to compare against; a real car is 'about a thousand kilograms', so 5 grams is instantly absurd.

Recap, and the theme of this whole sub-topic: carry units always, check them with dimensional analysis, report only the significant figures your data supports, state your uncertainty with a ±, and sanity-check the size of every answer. Honest numbers beat impressive-looking fake ones.

items.prompt: A label reads '100 g ± 2 g'. What does the '± 2 g' part mean?
choices: The weight is exactly 100 g with no doubt
The give-or-take: the true weight is probably between 98 g and 102 g
The scale is broken
You should add 2 g to every reading
items.explanation: The '± 2 g' is the uncertainty, the give-or-take, telling you the true value most likely sits between 98 g and 102 g. It is not a claim of perfect exactness, not a sign the scale is broken, and not an instruction to add 2 g; it honestly states how much wiggle room the measurement has.

items.prompt: Why is it worth reporting an uncertainty (a ± range) at all, instead of just a single number?
choices: Because it makes the number look more complicated
Because it is required only for very large numbers
Because no measurement is perfectly exact, so the ± honestly shows how much doubt there is
Because the ± makes the measurement exact
items.explanation: Every real measurement has some doubt, and the ± range communicates that honestly so others know how far to trust the value. It is not about looking complicated or reserved for big numbers, and it does not make anything exact, it does the opposite, openly admitting the give-or-take.

items.prompt: How does uncertainty connect to the random scatter from the previous sub-topic?
choices: Random scatter in repeated readings becomes a ± range, and averaging several readings shrinks it
Random scatter has nothing to do with uncertainty
Scatter makes a measurement exact
Averaging readings makes the scatter larger
items.explanation: The wobble in repeated readings is exactly what a ± range captures, and averaging several readings reduces that scatter, shrinking the range. Scatter is the source of uncertainty, not unrelated to it; it never makes a measurement exact, and averaging reduces scatter rather than enlarging it.

items.prompt: You add two bags of flour, each 100 g ± 2 g. What is the honest way to think about the total?
choices: Exactly 200 g with zero give-or-take
About 200 g, but the give-or-takes combine, so the total carries doubt too
100 g, because the uncertainties cancel out
The total has no uncertainty as long as each bag did
items.explanation: The amounts add to about 200 g, and because each input had a give-or-take, the total inherits doubt as well, when you add quantities, uncertainties combine. The total is not magically exact, the uncertainties do not cancel to zero, and the result cannot be more certain than the inputs it came from.

items.prompt: In plain words, what happens to certainty when you combine several noisy measurements?
choices: The combined answer becomes perfectly certain
The answer is no more certain than its inputs; combining noisy inputs gives a noisier result
Noise always cancels completely when you combine values
Certainty only depends on the first measurement you took
items.explanation: A result inherits the doubt of its parts, so combining noisy inputs yields an answer that is at least as noisy, never more certain than the inputs. Combining does not create perfect certainty, noise does not reliably cancel to nothing, and every input matters, not just the first.

items.prompt: A calculation tells you a family car weighs 5 grams. What is the right reaction?
choices: Trust it, since the math was done on a calculator
Report it as 5.0000 grams to be precise
The SIZE is absurd, so a unit or decimal point slipped; recheck the work
Accept it but add a ± range
items.explanation: A real car is roughly a thousand kilograms, so 5 grams is wildly the wrong size, a clear sign a unit or decimal slipped, and the fix is to recheck, not to trust it. Adding decimal places or a ± range does not rescue an answer whose order of magnitude is plainly impossible.

items.prompt: What is a 'sanity check' (order-of-magnitude estimate) used for?
choices: To get the exact answer to many decimal places
To replace careful measurement entirely
To make a result look more impressive
To check quickly whether an answer's SIZE makes sense, catching big blunders
items.explanation: A sanity check is a rough order-of-magnitude estimate whose job is to confirm the answer is roughly the right size, catching slipped units or decimals. It is not meant to give an exact, many-digit result, it does not replace real measurement, and it is about catching errors, not impressing anyone.

items.prompt: What is a 'Fermi estimate'?
choices: A precise measurement taken with a specialized instrument
A rough, common-sense approximation (a back-of-envelope guess) used to get a ballpark
A rule that forbids rounding
A type of unit like meters or grams
items.explanation: A Fermi estimate is a quick, rough, common-sense guess, a back-of-envelope ballpark, useful for sanity-checking whether an answer's size is reasonable. It is deliberately not a precise instrument reading, it is not a rounding rule, and it is not a unit; its value is speed and rough scale, not exactness.

items.prompt: A trip across town supposedly takes 0.001 seconds. A quick estimate says crossing a town takes minutes. What does this tell you?
choices: The 0.001 seconds is correct because the estimate is just a guess
You should report the time with more decimal places
The estimate proves the SIZE is wrong, so a unit or decimal slipped and the result needs rechecking
Trips across town really can take 0.001 seconds
items.explanation: A common-sense estimate puts a cross-town trip at minutes, so 0.001 seconds is the wrong size by a huge margin, signalling a slipped unit or decimal that must be rechecked. The rough estimate beats the absurd precise-looking figure here, and extra decimal places cannot fix an answer whose scale is impossible.

items.prompt: Pulling the whole sub-topic together, which statement best captures handling numbers honestly?
choices: Carry units always, check them with dimensional analysis, keep only the significant figures your data supports, state uncertainty with a ±, and sanity-check the size of every answer
Show as many decimal places as the calculator gives, and skip units to save space
A number without a unit is fine, and uncertainty never needs reporting
Round early and often, and trust any answer the calculator produces
items.explanation: Honest numbers come from carrying units, checking them with dimensional analysis, reporting only the sig figs the data supports, stating a ± uncertainty, and sanity-checking the size, the through-line of this sub-topic. The other options repeat the exact traps covered: faking precision with calculator digits, dropping units, hiding uncertainty, and rounding early.

lessons.title: The Mindset: Find the Cause by Evidence, Not Guessing
lessons.content:
Your car won't start. The natural urge is to poke at random: jiggle a wire, try the key again, maybe kick a tire. That **panic-poking** wastes time and often makes things worse. There is a calmer, faster way.

The core idea of **troubleshooting** (the act of finding why something is broken so you can fix it) is simple: a **fault** (something not working as it should) always has a *logical cause*. Nothing breaks by magic. Your job is to find that cause by **evidence** -- real observations -- not by guessing. Think of a detective: they follow clues to the culprit instead of accusing people at random.

Two habits sit at the heart of this mindset. First, **reproduce the problem**: can you make it happen on demand? A problem you can trigger reliably is far easier to fix than an **intermittent** one (a problem that comes and goes). So note its exact conditions -- when does it happen, and when does it *not*? "The engine only stalls when it's cold" is a huge clue, because it tells you where to look.

Second, **check the simple stuff first**. Before assuming something exotic is wrong, confirm the obvious: Is it plugged in? Is it switched on? Is there fuel, power, water? This is the famous "have you tried turning it off and on again?" -- not a joke, but the cheapest first test.

And resist the temptation to change five things at once. If you do, and the problem goes away, you won't know which change fixed it.

**Recap:** a fault has a logical cause; find it with evidence, not random pokes. Reproduce it, note its exact conditions, and check the simple stuff first.

items.prompt: What is the core mindset of good troubleshooting?
choices: A fault has a logical cause that you find by gathering evidence
Faults are mostly random, so just keep changing things until one works
The first thing you suspect is almost always correct
If a problem comes and goes, ignore it rather than study it
items.explanation: Good troubleshooting assumes every fault has a logical cause that evidence can reveal, like a detective following clues. Changing things at random is panic-poking, trusting your first suspect ignores evidence, and ignoring an on-and-off problem throws away the clues hidden in its conditions.

items.prompt: Your lamp won't turn on. Which response best fits the troubleshooting mindset?
choices: Buy a whole new lamp immediately without checking anything
Calmly check the obvious first, like whether it is plugged in and switched on
Rewire the lamp, change the bulb, and move it to a new room all at once
Decide it is probably the wiring in the wall and stop looking further
items.explanation: Checking the simple stuff first -- plug, switch, power -- is the cheapest, fastest test and fits the calm, evidence-first mindset. Buying a new lamp skips finding the cause, changing many things at once tangles cause and effect, and committing to the wall wiring with no evidence is guessing.

items.prompt: Why is it usually a mistake to change five things at once when trying to fix a problem?
choices: Because changing things is always forbidden during troubleshooting
Because it is slower than doing nothing at all
Because if the problem goes away, you won't know which change actually fixed it
Because the more changes you make, the more reliable the result becomes
items.explanation: If several changes happen together and the fault disappears, you cannot tell which one was responsible, so you have learned nothing you can trust or repeat. Changing things is allowed -- just one at a time; it is not slower than inaction; and piling on changes makes results less clear, not more reliable.

items.prompt: What does it mean to 'reproduce' a problem?
choices: To make the problem happen on demand so you can study it
To photocopy the instructions that came with the device
To replace the broken item with an identical new one
To describe the problem to a friend and leave it at that
items.explanation: Reproducing means being able to trigger the problem whenever you want, which lets you observe it and test fixes. It is not about copying paperwork, swapping in a new item, or merely talking about the issue; the point is to make the fault appear reliably so you can examine it.

items.prompt: A car 'only stalls when the engine is cold.' Why is noticing that condition so valuable?
choices: It proves the car can never be fixed
It is irrelevant, since the conditions of a fault don't matter
It means you should ignore the stalling until it happens randomly
It is a strong clue that narrows down where the cause could be
items.explanation: Knowing the exact condition under which a fault appears points you toward its cause and rules out a lot of possibilities. The condition does not make the car unfixable, conditions matter a great deal, and ignoring the problem wastes the very clue that 'only when cold' hands you.

items.prompt: Why is a reliably reproducible problem usually easier to fix than an intermittent one?
choices: Because reproducible problems are always less serious
Because you can trigger it whenever you want, so you can observe it and test fixes
Because intermittent problems fix themselves if you wait
Because a reproducible problem needs no evidence to solve
items.explanation: If you can make the fault happen on demand, you can watch exactly what occurs and immediately see whether a change helped. Reproducible does not mean less serious, intermittent faults rarely cure themselves, and you still gather evidence -- being reproducible just makes that evidence far easier to collect.

items.prompt: Your Wi-Fi is down. Which action best reflects 'check the simple stuff first'?
choices: Immediately call an engineer to rewire your whole house
Assume your internet provider has permanently failed and give up
Confirm the router is powered on and the cables are plugged in, then try restarting it
Change your router, your laptop, and your provider all on the same day
items.explanation: Confirming power and connections and trying a restart are the cheapest, most common fixes, so they come first. Calling for a rewire skips the obvious checks, assuming a permanent provider failure is a guess with no evidence, and changing everything at once makes it impossible to know what helped.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best captures the troubleshooting mindset?
choices: A detective following clues to the culprit, not accusing people at random
A gambler hoping the next random guess pays off
A shopper buying replacements until something works
A person turning away from a problem because it is confusing
items.explanation: Troubleshooting is detective work: you let evidence lead you to the real cause rather than accusing at random. Gambling on guesses, buying replacements blindly, and avoiding the problem all abandon the patient, clue-following approach that finds faults fast.

items.prompt: A friend says, 'It's broken sometimes and works other times, so there's no point trying to figure out when -- I'll just keep poking at it.' What is the better approach?
choices: Agree, since an on-and-off problem can't be studied
Change as many parts as possible at once to cover all bases
Note exactly when it works and when it fails, to turn the random fault into a clue
Trust the very first cause that comes to mind and act on it
items.explanation: Recording when an intermittent fault appears and when it doesn't turns a frustrating on-and-off problem into useful evidence about its cause. An intermittent fault can absolutely be studied, changing many parts at once tangles cause and effect, and seizing the first theory you think of is guessing rather than gathering evidence.

lessons.title: Divide and Conquer: Isolate the Fault One Change at a Time
lessons.content:
Suppose the lamp still won't light. The suspects form a chain: the **bulb**, the **lamp** itself, the **outlet** (the wall socket), and the **breaker** (the household switch that cuts power to a circuit). Checking each one blindly is slow. There is a much faster technique.

It is called **divide and conquer** (also "isolating the fault"): split the system in half and test which half holds the problem, then split that half again. Each test roughly halves the number of suspects, so you zero in fast instead of inspecting everything. Computer scientists call this idea a **binary search**; in plain words, it is the high-low guessing game -- each guess cuts the remaining possibilities in half.

The most powerful tool for this is **known-good substitution**: swap in a part you *trust* to test a suspect. Put a bulb you know works into the dead lamp. If it lights, the old bulb was the fault. If it stays dark, the bulb was fine -- cross it off and move further down the chain. Plug another device into the same outlet: if that device works, the outlet is fine, so suspicion shifts back to the lamp.

Tying it all together is one golden rule: **change one thing at a time, and observe**. Make a single change, watch its effect, and keep a simple record of what you tried and what happened. If you tweak several things together, you tangle cause and effect and can't tell what mattered. A cook testing a recipe changes one ingredient per batch for exactly this reason.

**Recap:** divide the system in half to isolate the fault, use a known-good part to test each suspect, and change just one thing at a time so every result has a clear meaning.

items.prompt: What is 'divide and conquer' (isolating the fault) in troubleshooting?
choices: Replacing every part of the system at the same time
Splitting the system in half to find which half holds the fault, then splitting again
Asking someone else to deal with the whole problem
Guessing the cause and buying that part right away
items.explanation: Divide and conquer means halving the system, testing which half contains the fault, and repeating, so the suspects shrink fast. Replacing everything at once is the opposite of isolating, handing it off avoids the technique, and guessing-then-buying skips the systematic narrowing that makes this method powerful.

items.prompt: Why does halving the system on each test help you find a fault quickly?
choices: Because it removes the need to observe anything
Because changing many parts at once is always faster
Because each test roughly cuts the number of suspects in half
Because the fault is always exactly in the middle
items.explanation: Each halving test eliminates about half the remaining suspects, so you converge on the culprit in just a few steps. It does not remove the need to observe results, changing many parts at once tangles cause and effect, and the fault need not sit in the middle -- the test simply tells you which half to keep searching.

items.prompt: What is 'known-good substitution'?
choices: Throwing away any part you suspect without testing it
Swapping in a part you trust to test whether the suspect part is the fault
Buying the most expensive replacement part available
Changing several parts together to save time
items.explanation: Known-good substitution means putting in a part you are confident works to see whether the suspect was really to blame. Discarding parts untested wastes good components, price has nothing to do with it, and swapping several parts together would tangle cause and effect rather than isolate the one at fault.

items.prompt: You put a bulb you KNOW works into the dead lamp and it still won't light. What have you learned?
choices: The bulb was the problem all along
Nothing useful can be learned from this test
The bulb is fine, so you should look further down the chain (lamp, outlet, breaker)
You should now change the bulb, the lamp, and the outlet at once
items.explanation: A trusted bulb that still won't light clears the bulb of blame and points you onward to the lamp, outlet, or breaker. It cannot mean the original bulb was at fault, the test is genuinely informative, and changing three things at once would destroy the clarity this single substitution just gave you.

items.prompt: Why is 'change one thing at a time, and observe' such an important rule?
choices: Because making one change at a time means you never have to keep records
Because it guarantees the very first change will fix the problem
Because only one change is ever allowed for the entire repair
Because then each result has a clear meaning -- you can tell exactly what your change did
items.explanation: Changing a single thing and watching the effect lets you attribute the result to that one change, keeping cause and effect untangled. It does not remove the value of keeping records, it does not promise the first try works, and it does not limit you to one change total -- you make many changes, just one at a time.

items.prompt: A cook changes only one ingredient per batch when testing a recipe. Which troubleshooting habit does this match?
choices: Changing one thing at a time so you can tell what caused the difference
Guessing the result without tasting anything
Fixing the plate's appearance instead of the flavor
Ignoring batches that turn out inconsistent
items.explanation: Altering a single ingredient per batch is exactly change-one-thing-at-a-time: it isolates the effect of that ingredient. Skipping the taste is guessing without evidence, fixing appearance over flavor is treating a symptom, and ignoring inconsistent batches throws away clues -- none of which is what the careful cook is doing.

items.prompt: The high-low guessing game -- where each guess cuts the remaining numbers in half -- is a plain-words picture of which technique?
choices: Changing many variables at once
Divide and conquer (binary search) to isolate the fault
Fixing the symptom rather than the cause
Trusting your first theory without testing
items.explanation: Halving the possibilities with each guess is the binary-search idea, which is exactly how divide and conquer isolates a fault. It is not about changing many variables together, not about patching symptoms, and not about clinging to a first guess; it is systematic narrowing, one informative test at a time.

items.prompt: Your phone won't charge. Using known-good substitution, what is a strong first test?
choices: Replace the phone, the cable, and the wall charger all at the same time
Try a charging cable you know works, changing only that one part
Decide the phone's battery is dead and stop investigating
Charge it in five different rooms before changing anything
items.explanation: Swapping in a cable you trust -- and only the cable -- tests one suspect cleanly: if it charges, the old cable was the fault. Replacing everything at once tangles the result, declaring the battery dead is a guess with no evidence, and trying many rooms changes nothing about the suspected parts.

items.prompt: A teammate says, 'Let's just swap the bulb, the cord, and the outlet together -- one of them is bound to be it.' Why is this weaker than divide and conquer?
choices: It isn't weaker; changing everything at once is the fastest method
Because the bulb is always the real cause anyway
Because even if it works, you won't know which part was actually at fault
Because you should never replace a part during troubleshooting
items.explanation: Swapping several parts together may get the lamp working, but it leaves you unable to say which part was broken -- so cause and effect stay tangled. Changing everything at once is not the fastest reliable method, the bulb is not automatically the cause, and replacing parts is fine when you do it one at a time to isolate the fault.

lessons.title: Test Cheapest First, Fix the Root Cause, Then Verify
lessons.content:
You have a method; now make it efficient and trap-proof. A **hypothesis** is just an educated guess at the cause. The smart order is to test the **most likely and cheapest-to-check** hypotheses first. For a flopped recipe, "maybe I misread the oven temperature" is quick to check, so test it before tearing the oven apart. If a guess is wrong, cross it off and move on. Crucially, **don't fall in love with your first theory** -- the urge to keep defending an early guess even as evidence piles up against it is called **confirmation bias**, and it is the classic way smart people waste hours. A good doctor rules causes out one by one; so should you.

Now the deepest idea: fix the **root cause**, not the symptom. The **symptom** is the visible effect; the **root cause** is the real underlying reason behind it. Mopping a puddle treats the symptom; the puddle returns until you fix the leaking pipe -- the root cause. A simple tool to dig down is the **"5 whys"**: keep asking *why* until you reach the real reason. Floor is wet -- why? A pipe drips -- why? A joint is loose. Tighten the joint and the puddle is gone for good.

Finally, two endgame habits that beginners skip. **Verify the fix**: confirm the problem is actually gone *and* that you didn't break something else. And **don't leave failed changes in**: if a change didn't help, undo it, or you leave a mess that confuses the next round.

**Recap:** test the cheapest likely causes first, avoid confirmation bias, fix the root cause rather than the symptom (use the 5 whys), then verify the fix and undo anything that didn't help.

items.prompt: When you have several possible causes (hypotheses), which should you usually test first?
choices: The most likely and cheapest-to-check cause
The most expensive and time-consuming one, to get it over with
Whichever one you find most interesting
All of them at once, so you don't have to think about order
items.explanation: Testing the most likely and cheapest causes first finds many faults quickly with the least effort. Starting with the costliest option wastes time, picking the most interesting ignores likelihood, and testing everything at once tangles cause and effect so you can't tell which guess was right.

items.prompt: What is the difference between a symptom and a root cause?
choices: They are two words for exactly the same thing
The symptom is the visible effect; the root cause is the real underlying reason behind it
The root cause is what you see; the symptom is hidden
The symptom is always more important to fix than the root cause
items.explanation: The symptom is the effect you notice, while the root cause is the deeper reason that keeps producing it. They are not the same thing, the labels are not reversed, and fixing only the symptom leaves the root cause to bring the problem back -- which is why the root cause is what you ultimately want to fix.

items.prompt: Mopping up a puddle again and again while the pipe keeps leaking is an example of what mistake?
choices: Reproducing the problem too carefully
Testing the cheapest hypothesis first
Fixing the symptom instead of the root cause
Changing only one thing at a time
items.explanation: Mopping clears the visible puddle (the symptom) but the leak (the root cause) refills it, so the fix never lasts. It is not over-careful reproduction, not cheap-first testing, and not single-variable changing -- it is the classic trap of treating the symptom while the real cause goes untouched.

items.prompt: What does the '5 whys' technique help you do?
choices: Decide how many people to blame for a problem
Keep asking 'why' until you reach the real underlying cause
Count exactly five separate problems before fixing any
Prove that the first cause you thought of was right
items.explanation: The 5 whys means repeatedly asking why -- floor wet, pipe drips, joint loose -- until you hit the root cause you can actually fix. It is not about assigning blame, it is not a rule to find exactly five problems, and it is meant to dig past your first guess rather than confirm it.

items.prompt: What is 'confirmation bias' in troubleshooting?
choices: Carefully writing down each test and its result
Checking the simple, obvious causes before the complicated ones
Splitting the system in half to isolate the fault
Clinging to your first theory and ignoring evidence that it's wrong
items.explanation: Confirmation bias is favoring your first guess and brushing aside evidence against it, which can waste hours chasing the wrong cause. Recording tests, checking simple causes first, and dividing the system are all sound habits -- the trap is falling in love with an early theory rather than following the evidence.

items.prompt: After you think you've fixed a problem, why should you verify the fix?
choices: To confirm the problem is truly gone and that you didn't break something else
Because verifying is only needed if someone is watching
To make the repair take as long as possible
Because a fix you believe in never needs checking
items.explanation: Verifying confirms the fault is actually gone and that your change didn't cause a new problem, which is the only way to know you're really done. Verification isn't for show, it isn't about stalling, and confidence is not proof -- even a fix you trust must be checked against reality.

items.prompt: You tried a change, it didn't help, and you left it in place anyway before trying the next idea. Why is leaving failed changes in a trap?
choices: It isn't a problem; leftover changes never cause confusion
Because leftover changes pile up and confuse the next round of testing
Because undoing a change is against the rules of troubleshooting
Because the more leftover changes there are, the clearer the cause becomes
items.explanation: Failed changes left in place stack up and muddy later tests, so you can no longer tell what is affecting the system. Leftover changes do cause confusion, undoing them is encouraged rather than forbidden, and more clutter makes the cause harder to see, not clearer.

items.prompt: A recipe flopped. Which approach best fits 'test the cheapest likely cause first and avoid confirmation bias'?
choices: Insist it must be a bad oven and replace the oven before anything else
Quickly recheck the oven temperature and your measurements, and be ready to drop a guess that proves wrong
Assume your favorite theory is right and stop looking once you've named it
Change the recipe, the oven, and the pan all at once and hope
items.explanation: Rechecking temperature and measurements is fast and likely, and staying ready to abandon a wrong guess is the cure for confirmation bias. Replacing the oven first is expensive and unproven, locking onto a favorite theory is the bias itself, and changing everything at once tangles cause and effect.

items.prompt: Which sequence best summarizes calm, systematic troubleshooting from start to finish?
choices: Guess a cause, defend it no matter what, and ship the repair without checking
Change many things at once, fix the symptom, and skip verifying
Reproduce it, gather evidence, divide and conquer, change one thing at a time, fix the root cause, then verify
Ignore the conditions, mop the symptom, and leave failed changes in place
items.explanation: The full method is reproduce, gather evidence, divide and conquer to isolate, change one thing at a time, fix the root cause, and verify -- each step building on real observations. The other options bundle the classic traps: defending a first guess, changing many things at once, patching symptoms, ignoring conditions, and skipping verification.

lessons.title: Verification vs Validation: Right vs the Right Thing
lessons.content:
Imagine a family hires a builder and hands over a blueprint. The builder follows it perfectly: every wall, every measurement, exactly as drawn. The house matches the plan flawlessly. Then the family moves in and discovers it does not fit their lives at all. There are stairs everywhere, but grandma uses a wheelchair. The house was built **right**, yet it was the **wrong thing** to build.

That story holds the single most important idea in this lesson. Once you build something, building it is not enough. You have to prove it **works**, and prove it solves the real need. Engineers split that proof into two questions.

**Verification** asks: *did we build the thing right?* In plain terms, does it match the plan, the **spec** (short for specification, the written list of what it must do), the requirements you wrote down? Checking the house against the blueprint is verification.

**Validation** asks: *did we build the right thing?* Does it actually solve the user's real need in the real world? Asking whether this is the house the family actually needed is validation.

Here is the catch that surprises everyone: you can pass verification and still fail validation. If the blueprint itself was wrong, you can build it perfectly (verified) and still hand over something nobody wanted (not validated). Building it isn't the finish line. Proving it is right, and that it is the right thing, is.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what question does VERIFICATION ask?
choices: Did we build the thing right (does it meet the spec)?
Did we build the right thing (does it solve the real need)?
Did we build it as cheaply as possible?
Did the customer enjoy using it?
items.explanation: Verification asks whether you built the thing right: does it match the spec and the requirements you wrote down? Asking whether it solves the real need is validation, a different question. Cost and enjoyment are separate concerns; verification is specifically about meeting the agreed specification.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what question does VALIDATION ask?
choices: Does every measurement match the blueprint exactly?
Did we build the right thing (does it actually solve the user's real need)?
Did we follow the written spec line by line?
Did we finish on the planned date?
items.explanation: Validation asks whether you built the right thing: does it truly meet the user's real need? Matching the blueprint and following the spec line by line are both verification. Finishing on time is about scheduling. Validation is the test of whether the thing was worth building at all.

items.prompt: A builder finishes a house that matches the blueprint perfectly, but the family realizes it is not the home they actually needed. Which statement is true?
choices: It passed validation but failed verification
It failed both verification and validation
It passed verification but failed validation
Verification and validation both mean the same thing here
items.explanation: Matching the blueprint means it was built right, so it passed verification. But it does not meet the family's real need, so it failed validation. This is the classic 'right vs right thing' gap: you can build perfectly to a spec that was itself wrong. The two terms are genuinely different questions, not synonyms.

items.prompt: A phone app is checked against its written requirements: every listed feature is present and behaves as the spec says. Which activity is this?
choices: Validation, because it checks the real user need
Verification, because it checks the app against its spec
Neither, because checking a spec is not a real test
Both at once, since any check covers need and spec together
items.explanation: Checking the app against its written spec is verification: did we build the thing right?
